titleZh: 第一讲：6000 代以前的人类长什么样?

paragraphs.en: Hello! And welcome to our lecture series on World History. So I would like to start this series by asking a simple question: 'Where do we come from?' This question has two answers: a simple answer and a bigger answer. The simple answer is that we come from our parents. Here is a picture of me when I was very small. I am standing with my mom and dad. The bigger answer is that we come from our ancestors.
paragraphs.zh: 大家好！欢迎来到世界历史系列讲 座。 那么在讲座的一开始，我想问一个 简单的问题： “我们是从哪里来的？” 这个问题有两个答案：一个简单 的，一个宽泛的。 简单的答案就是我们是父母生的。 这是一张我很小时候的照片。我站 在我父母的身边。 宽泛的答案就是我们是先祖的后 代。

paragraphs.en: Ancestors are the parents of our parents of our parents, going back and back through the generations. I also like to think about my ancestors as my family in the past. So let's start this discussion of the past by thinking about ancestors. We will use my family as an example. We will start with me and go back and back, through the generations, thinking about what each ancestor experienced in his or her lifetime. So here I am, James, your teacher. I was born in 1985, so I am now 33 years old. In my lifetime I have experienced historical events like the invention of smart phones, the financial crisis,
paragraphs.zh: 先祖就是我们父母的父母的父母， 这样一代一代往前数。 我也喜欢把先祖看作我很久以前 的家人。 那么我们就从先祖这个话题展开 这次讨论。 我们会用我的家族作为例子。 我们会从我本人开始，一代一代地 往前追溯，想一想每一位先祖在他 或她的时代都经历了什么。 这就是我了，詹姆斯，你们的老师。 我生于 1985 年，所以我今年 33 岁。 我有生以来，经历过诸如智能手机 的发明、金融危机、伊拉克战争、 互联网问世这些历史事件。

paragraphs.en: the Iraq war, and the beginning of the internet. Now, let's go back a generation to my mother. Her name is Corinna. Of course she experienced all the things that I did, but she has also experienced many events that happened before me. My mom was alive to see historical events like the Vietnam War, The Moon Landing, The Civil Rights Movement and The Invention of the Personal Computer. Now let's go back another generation to my mother's father, my grandfather. His name was Harvey. He was born in 1923. That is 95 years ago. In his life my grandfather saw many events like World War 2., the Great
paragraphs.zh: 现在，我们回到上一代，我母亲这 一辈。她名叫科琳娜。 当然，我经历的事件她都经历过， 但是她还经历过许多在我出生以 前发生的事件。 我母亲见证了像越南战争、人类登 月、民权运动和个人计算机的发明 这些历史事件。 现在，我们再向前一代，来到我母 亲的父亲，我的外祖父这里。 他名叫哈维，生于 1923 年，也就 是 95 年前。 我的外祖父一生中经历过很多历 史事件，比如二战、“大萧条”，

paragraphs.en: Depression, and the invention of Television. But we won't stop there. Let us go back another generation to Harvey's mother, my Great-Grandmother. Her name was Dorothy and she was born in 1897. That is 122 years ago. In her life, my Great-Grandmother saw many important historical events like the invention of airplanes, World War 1, The sinking of the Titanic, and the invention of radio. Dorothy's father was a man named Freeman Worthington Butts. He is my great-great-grandfather. He was born in 1874. That is 145 years ago. In his life, Freeman saw the first cars, the first light-bulbs, the first Skyscrapers,
paragraphs.zh: 还有电视机的问世。 但是我们不会就此停住脚步。让我 们再往前数一代，看看哈维的母 亲，我的外曾祖母。 她叫多萝西 ，生于 1897 年，距 今 122 年。 我的外曾祖母一生经历了很多重 大历史事件，比如飞机的诞生、一 战、泰坦尼克号沉没，还有无线电 的发明。 多萝西的父亲名叫弗里曼，他是我 的外曾曾外祖父。他生于 145 年前 的 1874 年。 弗里曼一生中见证了第一辆汽车、 第一盏电灯、第一幢摩天大楼、

paragraphs.en: the first electric power plants, and the first telephones. But let's keep going. Freeman's mother was a woman named Mary. She is my great-great-great-grandmother. She was born in 1849. That is 170 years ago. In her life, Mary saw the American Civil War, The First Railroad across America, and The settlement of the Western United States. Mary's mother was a woman named Maria. She is my great-great-great-great-grandmother She was born in 1817, that is 202 years ago.
paragraphs.zh: 第一座发电厂，还有第一部电话的 问世。 让我们继续往前走。弗里曼的母亲 名叫玛丽。他是我的外曾曾外曾祖 母。 她生于 1849 年，那是 170 年前了。 玛丽一生中见证了美国内战、第一 条横跨美国的铁路竣工、以及美国 西部的开发。 但是还没完呢。我们再往前追溯一 代。玛丽的母亲名叫玛利亚。 她是我的外曾曾外曾曾外祖母。 她生于 1817 年，距今 202 年。

paragraphs.en: In her life, Maria saw events like the Very First Trains, the Invention of Cameras, the Invention of the Telegraph, and the First Steam-ships. Let's see, if six generations takes us back 200 years, what happens if we go back 400 years, or twice as far into the past. Going back 400 years requires 13 generations.13 generatinons back,and we come to the life of John Howland. John was born in 1591, that is 428 years ago. This means that John is my great-great-great-great-great-great-g reat-great-great-great-great-grandfat her! John is an interesting ancestor,
paragraphs.zh: 在玛利亚的一生中，她见证过第一 列火车、第一台照相机、第一封电 报，还有第一艘蒸汽轮船。 我们看一看，如果六代人把我们带 回到了 200 年以前，那么 400 年、 乃至 800 年前会是怎样呢？ 400 年前，大概 13 代人的时间。往 前数 13 代，我们就来到了 John Howland 的时代 约翰生于 1591 年，距今 428 年。 这意味着约翰是我的外曾曾曾 曾……祖父！ 约翰是一位有趣的先祖，因为他是

paragraphs.en: because he was a passenger onboard the Mayflower. These passengers founded Plymouth colony, which became the first English colony in the Northern United States. John was there at the very beginning of the history of the United States. During the trip from England to America, there was a big storm, and John fell overboard. Fortunately,the other people on the Mayflower threw him a rope.He caught the rope and then they pulled him back onboad,saving his life. So if John had let go of that rope, I would never have existed. In many ways,we can think of the Mayflower’s as the beginning of the
paragraphs.zh: 五月花号的乘客之一。 这群乘客建立了普利茅斯殖民地， 这里成为了美国北方第一块英国 殖民地。 约翰正是生活在美国历史的发端。 在从英国到美洲的旅途中，船只遭 遇了大风暴，约翰不幸坠海。 幸运的是，“五月花号”上的其他 乘客抛给他一条绳子。他抓住绳 子，被拉回船上，捡回了一条命。 所以要是约翰没抓住那根绳子，也 就没有我了。 很大意义上，我们可以把“五月花 号”看做是美国历史的开端。

paragraphs.en: history of the United States. There are 12 generations between me and John Howland. So we can say, that the history of the English in America is 12 generations long. But remember, the Native Americans, have been in America for 15,000 years. So their history goes back for hundreds of generations. So 12 generations brings us back 400 years. But what if we go back five times as far and go 60 generations into the past? I lose track of my own family after 12 generations,but I can tell you what is happening in the world.
paragraphs.zh: 在我和约翰之间，隔着 12 代。 所以我们可以说，英国人在美洲创 造的历史有 12 代人那么长。 但是请记住，美洲的原住民已经在 这里生活了 15,000 年。所以他们 的历史可以往前追溯几百代人了。 12 代意味着 400 年。 如果我们往回倒 60 代呢？ 对于 12 代以前的家庭史，我已经 不得而知，但我可以告诉大家，12 代以前甚至更久远的世界是什么 样的。

paragraphs.en: This is what 60 generations looks like. 60 generations is 2000 years into the past. 60 generations ago the Roman Empire ruled the Mediterranean. The Colosseum is about 60 generations old. 60 generations ago, China was ruled by the Han Dynasty. This fort was built during the Han dyasty. 60 generations ago, Rome was ruled by Ceasar Augustus, and China was ruled by Liu Xiu. Okay, so sixty generations takes us back 2000 years. But what if we go ten times further back to 600 generations.
paragraphs.zh: 60 代人看起来是这样的。 60 代人也就是 2000 年前。 上溯 60 代人，罗马帝国统治着地 中海。 古罗马斗兽场有大约 60 代人的历 史。 60 代人以前，中国正处在汉王朝。 这是汉朝建立的城池。 60 代人以前，凯撒·奥古斯都统治 着罗马，而汉光武帝刘秀统治着中 国。 好了，所以说 60 代人把我们带回 了 2000 年前。 那么如果我们往前追溯10倍时间， 到 600 代以前呢？

paragraphs.en: This is what 600 generations looks like. 600 generations brings us back to 20,000 years ago. 20,000 years ago, the Earth was in the middle of the last ice age. 20,000 years ago, Wooly mammoths, Wooly rhinos, and saber-toothed tigers all roamed the earth. In those days all humans lived in small bands of 10 to 50 people. They survived by hunting animals and gathering plants. 20,000 years ago, our ancestors still hunted wooly mammoths for food. Farming, Towns, Writing, all of these things were still thousands of years in the future.
paragraphs.zh: 600 代人看起来是这样的。 600 代人把我们带到了 20,000 年 前。 20,000 年前，地球正处于最后一次 冰河时代之中。 20,000 年前，猛犸象、长毛犀牛， 以及剑齿虎还在大地上漫步。 那时，所有人类都聚居在 10 到 50 人一伙的小型群落中。 他们靠狩猎和采集维持生存。 20,000 年前，我们的祖先还在捕猎 猛犸象作为食物。 耕种、城镇、文字，这些事物要到 数千年之后才会出现。

paragraphs.en: But the history of our ancestors goes farther back than this. 600 generations takes us back to 20,000 years in the past. But what if we go ten times farther back, to 6,000 generations. Here is what 6,000 generations looks like. 6,000 generations takes us back 200,000 years into the past. 200,000 years ago is the very beginning of our species- Homo Sapiens. By 200,000 years ago we all have the same ancestors. The stories of your family and my family and the family of every person on Earth have all merged.
paragraphs.zh: 但是我们祖先的历史还要比这久 远很多。 600 代人把我们带回 20,000 年前。 要是再往追溯前十倍的时间， 到 了 6,000 代人以前呢？ 6,000 代人看起来是这样的。 6,000代人把我们带回到了20万年 前。 20 万年前，我们这个物种——智人 登上了历史舞台。 20 万年前我们都有着共同的祖先 你我还有地球上每一个人的家族 故事其实是融合在一起的。

paragraphs.en: And 200,000 years ago, these ancestors all lived in Africa. Back then we lived in small groups, hunting and gathering to survive. There were very few humans alive on Earth back then. There were probably fewer than 50,000 people on the whole Earth. Every human alive could have fit in workers stadium here in Beijing. But now I have a question. How far back can we go and still find one of our ancesors? Well，science has an answer. The oldest ancestor we know of goes back 200,000 generations to 4 million years ago.
paragraphs.zh: 而 20 万年前，这些祖先全都生活 在非洲大陆。 那时我们聚居在小群落中，以狩猎 采集为生。 那时地球上的人类数目还很少。 全球也只有大约不到 5 万人。 北京的工人体育场就能容纳当时 所有的人类。 但是现在我有一个问题。 我们的寻根溯源之旅，究竟能走多 远？ 科学可以给我们答案。 我们已知最早的祖先生活400万年 前，相隔 20 万代。

paragraphs.en: And here she is, Australopithecus Afarensis. The oldest ancestor that we know of. So please join me next class as we go back 4 million years, or 200,000 generations into the past. And we will talk about Australopithecus afarensis, the oldest human ancestor we know of. And the very beginning of our history. Thank you everybody.
paragraphs.zh: 这就是她了，阿法南方古猿，目前 已知最早的祖先。 所以，欢迎收听我的下一堂课，我 们会回到 400 万年前，或者说 20 万代人以前。 并且我们会讨论已知最早的人类 祖先，阿法南方古猿。 以及我们人类历史的开端。 谢谢大家。

titleZh: 第二讲：人类最古老的祖先：Lucy

paragraphs.en: Hello, and welcome back to our series on World History. Today we will be discussing Australopithecus, our most ancient ancestor. Another word for these ancient ancestors is Hominid. I will be using this word today. Our story begins in November 1974, in the country of Ethiopia. That year, a team of paleontologists and anthropologist were working in the Afar triangle, a hot dry desert in the corner of Ethiopia. The picture on the left should give you a sense of what the Afar triangle looks like. On November 24, 1974, Anthropologist Dr. Donald
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到我们的世界历史 系列讲座。今天我们将讨论南方古 猿，我们最古老的祖先。 另一个用来形容这些古代祖先的 词是“原始人类”。今天的课程中， 我将使用“原始人类”这个词。 我们的故事从1974年11月的埃塞 俄比亚开始。 那一年，一个由古生物学家和人类 学家组成的团队正好在阿法尔三 角地区工作，这是一个位于埃塞俄 比亚一角的炎热干燥的沙漠。 左边的图片展示了阿法尔三角地 区的地貌。 1974 年 11 月 24 日，人类学家唐 纳德·约翰森博士正在一个干燥的

paragraphs.en: Johansen was searching a dry gully for the bones of ancient human ancestors. He and his student Tom Gray had been searching all morning for fossils, but they had found nothing. Then, at around noon, Dr. Johansen decided to return to camp.As he was walking back to his car he just happened to glance over his shoulder, and he froze. His trained eye saw what looked like the elbow bones of an ancient hominid. He walked over to the bones and knelt down. As he looked around he saw other bones, another arm bone, a rib, knuckle bones. And he realized he wasn't looking at just one or two bones, he was looking at an entire skeleton.
paragraphs.zh: 沟壑中寻找远古人类祖先的遗骨。 他和他的学生汤姆格雷花了一整 个上午来寻找化石，他们什么也没 找到。 大约中午时分，约翰森博士决定返 回营地。当他往车上走去时，他只 是碰巧瞥了一眼他的肩膀方向，他 愣住了。 他看到了一种古老的原始人类的 肘部骨骼。 他走到骨头边上，跪了下来。他环 顾四周时，看到了其他的骨头：另 一根臂骨，一根肋骨，一根关节骨。 他意识到他看到的并不只是一两 根骨头，而是一整具骨架。

paragraphs.en: To understand what this means. You could go out for an entire season looking for ancient hominid bones, and if you found a single bone, that would be a success. If you found a skull, that could make your career, and here Dr. Johansen looking at an entire skeleton! If you put every single hominid fossil as old as that skeleton in one place, it would fit on this table. So Dr. Johansen and Tom Gray got in their car and raced back to camp. As they arrived Tom started honking the horn really loudly. And all the other scientists came running, excited. They asked him. Did you find something? And Tom jumped out of the car and said, 'We found the whole damn thing!'
paragraphs.zh: 这一发现意味着什么呢？举个例 子：你可以花整整三个月的时间， 去寻找古老的原始人类的骨头。如 果你找到了一根，就可以算是成功 了。如果你找到了一个头骨，你就 可以出人头地了。而约翰森博士看 到的，是一具完整的骨骼！ 而在当时，即便你将所有与这具骨 骼同一时期的古人类化石全收集 起来堆在一起，也仅仅能摆满这张 桌子而已。 于是，约翰森和汤姆·格雷驱车赶 往营地。一到营地，汤姆就开始大 声地按喇叭。所有其他的科学家都 来了，每个人都很兴奋。他们问他： “你找到了吗?”汤姆从车里跳出 来说：“我们找到了一整具骨骼！”

paragraphs.en: Within minutes every single scientist in that camp was on their way to the site. They spent the next three weeks combing that gully for every single bone they could find. And when they were done, they had retrieved 40% of a complete skeleton. They had discovered the oldest human ancestor known to science. Of course, after the first day of searching, the scientists all went back to their camp and celebrated. There was drinking and talking and great happiness. They knew they had just made a historic discovery. As they were partying they kept listening to the Beatles song: 'Lucy in the Sky with Diamond.' over and over again. And so the next day almost as a
paragraphs.zh: 没过几分钟，营地里的每一个科学 家都在去往现场的路上。接下来的 三个星期，每个人都在尽全力寻找 任何一块骨头。到任务结束时，他 们已经找到了 40%的完整骨架。 他们发现了科学界已知的最古老 的人类祖先。 在第一天的搜索之后科学家们都 回到了营地，他们当然要庆祝。他 们举杯欢庆，开怀畅饮，高谈阔论。 他们知道自己刚刚获得了一个历 史性的发现。 他们一边庆祝，一边单曲循环披头 士的名曲：《露西在钻石闪耀的天 空下》。 约翰森博士开玩笑似的提议把这

paragraphs.en: joke, Dr. Johansen suggested that they call the skeleton 'Lucy.' And the name stuck. Of course the formal name of the skeleton is Fossil AL-2881. But pretty soon all of the scientists, and then the whole world was calling her Lucy. Now, Lucy is an interesting creature. From the waist up, she is very much like an ape, very similar to a chimpanzee. In fact Australopithecus skulls have been discovered by other scientists after Lucy was discovered, and we now know that the Australopithecus brain was only 450 cubic centimeters in size. That's the same size as a chimpanzee brain. Human brains by contrast are 1400 cubic centimeters in size, about 3 times
paragraphs.zh: 具骨架叫做“露西”。这个名字就 这样一直流传了下来。 当然，这具骨骼的官方名称是 AL-2881 号化石。 但是很快，所有的科学家，随后全 世界都叫她露西。 露西很有意思，她看起来很像是 猿，类似黑猩猩。 事实上，在露西被发现后，又有其 他科学家发现了南方古猿的头骨。 现在我们知道南方古猿的大脑只 有 450 立方厘米大，和黑猩猩一 样。相比之下，人类大脑则是 1400 立方厘米，大约是南方古猿的 3 倍。

paragraphs.en: larger. So because of this, we think Australopithecus was about as intelligent as a chimpanzee, and probably behaved very much like a chimpanzee. But, from the waist down, Lucy was much more like us. You see, the shape of Lucy's hip and leg bones was much more like a human hip and leg bone. Chimpanzee legs point straight down from the hip, but human legs, and Australopithecus' legs point inwards a little bit. This means that when humans walk, our feet naturally land directly beneath us. You can see this if you draw a straight line on the ground. We
paragraphs.zh: 基于此，我们认为南方古猿的智力 水平与和黑猩猩相仿，行为表现也 很有可能与黑猩猩类似。 但是，腰部以下，露西更像我们人 类。 露西的髋骨和腿骨的形状更像人 类的髋骨和腿骨。 黑猩猩的腿从臀部垂直向下，但人 类和南方古猿的腿略微指向内侧。 这意味着，当人类走路时，我们的 脚自然地就落在身体下方。 如果你在地上画一条直线，你就能 注意到这一点。我们人类会自然地

paragraphs.en: humans will naturally walk along that line because our legs point inwards. But Chimpanzees have to wobble back and forth as the walk in order to keep their balance. So this brings up a question. Why did Australopithecus evolve the ability to walk on two legs？ What makes walking on two legs better than walking on four? So please join me next class, and I’ll tell you more about that. Thanks for your listening!
paragraphs.zh: 沿着这条线走，因为我们的腿指向 内部。 而黑猩猩为了保持平衡，就不得不 来回晃动。 这就引出了一个问题。 为什么南方古猿进化出了用两条 腿走路的能力？ 为什么用两腿走路比四腿走路更 好？ 请加入下一节课吧，我们会进一步 进行讨论。 谢谢！

titleZh: 第三讲：人为什么不用四条腿走路？

paragraphs.en: Hello everone, and welcome back. In our last class, I gave you guys the questions: Why did Australopithecus evolve the ability to walk on two legs. That is to say,what makes walking on two legs better than walking on four? The answer: Walking on two legs saves energy! We know this because of the work of two scientists: Herman Pontze at Washington University in St. Louis, and Daniel Lieberman at Harvard University. These two scientists decided to test how much energy chimpanzees and
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到我们的课堂。 在上一节课，我给大家留下了问 题： 为什么南方古猿进化出了用两条 腿走路的能力？ 也就是说，为什么两腿走路比四腿 走路更好？ 答案是： 两条腿走路更省力！ 我们能够知道这一点，归功于两位 科学家的研究：华盛顿大学的赫尔 曼·庞泽和哈佛大学的丹尼尔·利 伯曼。 这两位科学家决定测量黑猩猩和 人类行走时所消耗的能量。

paragraphs.en: humans use when they walk. To do this, the scientists trained several chimpanzees to walk on treadmills while wearing oxygen masks. As you can see in this picture. These masks could measure how much oxygen the chimpanzees were breathing. And by measuring the oxygen they were using, the sciencists could measure how much energy they were using when they walked. Then, the scientists repeated the same experiment with human volunteers. Since Chimpanzees can walk on two legs and four legs, I believe the scientists tested both to see if that made a difference. As far as I know it didn't. The chimpanzees used
paragraphs.zh: 为了做到这一点，科学家们训练了 几只黑猩猩戴着氧气面罩在跑步 机上行走。正如你在图中所见。 这些面罩测量黑猩猩在行走时呼 吸的氧气量。通过测量它们用掉的 氧气，科学家们可以测出它们在行 走时所消耗的能量。 然后，科学家们对人类志愿者做了 同样的实验。 由于黑猩猩既可以用两条腿走路， 也可以用四条腿，科学家们对这两 种情况进行了测试，以确定这对实 验结果是否有影响。答案是，没有 影响。黑猩猩用这两种方式行走时

paragraphs.en: similar amounts of energy with both methods. And this answer of the study was very interesting. Chimpanzees use about 50 kilocalories of energy to walk one kilometer. But humans only burn about 13 kilocalories to walk the same distance. We humans are 4 times more efficient at walking than chimpanzees! Okay, so we know that walking on two legs saves energy. But this creates another question: Why did Australopithecus need to more？ walk The answer that question,we need to
paragraphs.zh: 消耗的能量是差不多的。 结果非常有趣。 黑猩猩走完 1 公里需要消耗 50 千 卡的能量。 但人类只需要 13 千卡。 我们人类的行走效率是黑猩猩的4 倍！ 好了，现在我们知道，用两条腿走 路可以节省能量。 但这又产生了另一个问题： 为什么南方古猿需要多走路呢？ 回答这个问题，我们需要看看南方

paragraphs.en: think about where Australopthecus fits on the family tree of Great Apes. There are 5 different species of great apes that are living in the world today: Gorillas, Orangutans, Chimpanzees, Bonobos, and us humans. Here we have a family tree of the five species. As you can see, the species closest to us are the chimpanzees and the Bonobos. We share a common ancestor who lived 4 to 6 million years ago. Australopithecus lived after that common ancestor ape, around 3.5 million years ago. 3.5 million years ago, Humans and
paragraphs.zh: 古猿在“大猿”这个族群中所处的 位置。 现在世界上有 5 种不同种类的类 人猿： 大猩猩，猩猩，黑猩猩，倭黑猩猩 和我们人类。 现在，我们有了这五大类人猿的图 谱。 如你所见，离我们最近的物种是黑 猩猩和倭黑猩猩。 我们有共同的祖先，它们生活在 400 至 600 万年前。 南方古猿生活的年代要晚于比我 们共同的祖先猿，大约在 350 万年 前。 350 万年前，人类和黑猩猩已经分

paragraphs.en: Chimpanzees had already diverged from each other. Lucy is on the branch that leads to us. She is a human ancestor, she is NOT a chimpanzee ancestor. To find that common human-chimpanzee ancestor, we have to go back to 5 million years ago. This 'ancestor ape' has not yet been discovered. But we can guess that she probably was very similar to a chimpanzee and lived in a jungle environment where she had to climb trees all day long. We think this because both Chimpanzees and Australopithecus were very good climbers. But then an ice age began. Wait a minute, you might ask.
paragraphs.zh: 道扬镳。 露西处在通向人类的分支上，她是 人类的祖先，而不是黑猩猩的祖 先。 为了找到人类和黑猩猩共同的祖 先，我们需要追溯到 500 万年前。 这个“祖先猿”还没有被发现。 但我们可以猜测，她可能和黑猩猩 非常相似。因为生活在丛林环境 中，她需要经常爬树。我们之所以 这样认为，是因为黑猩猩和南方古 猿都是爬树专家。 但随后，冰河时代开始了。 你可能会想说，等一下。

paragraphs.en: How do we know the temperature millions of years ago? Well, one way scientist study this is by going to Antarctica. The glaciers are Antarctica are hundreds of thousand of years old. So they drill these deep holes into the glaciers and pull out these tubes of ice that they called 'ice cores'. These ice cores were formed by snow that fell hundreds of thousands of years ago. And the deeper you go into the glacier, the older the ice become. For instance, in this photographs the ice at the top was formed recently. But that dark band is from ice that is 21,000 years old. (The dark stuff is ash from a volcanic explosion.) And if you go further down, you get ice that's even older, maybe 100,000
paragraphs.zh: 我们怎么能知道几百万年前地球 的温度？ 科学家的研究方法之一是去南极 洲。南极洲的冰川有几十万年的历 史。 于是他们在冰川上钻出这些深孔， 取出这些被称为“冰芯”的冰管。 这些冰芯是由几十万年前的雪形 成的，钻得越深，冰的年代就越久 远。 例如，在这张照片里，顶部的冰是 最近形成的。但这条黑线来自于 21,000 年前。（黑色物质是火山 灰） 如果你钻得更深，你就能得到历史 更久远的冰，也许可以有 100,000

paragraphs.en: years old! Inside the ice are gases that have been trapped for hundreds of thousands of years. Scientists can perform some fancy chemistry on these gases and from the ratios of certain gases, they can know the average temperature at that time. From these ice cores scientists have been able to learn that there have been 5 periods in the last half a million years when Antarctica was much colder than it is today. From this they have learned that the Earth has gone through 5 ice ages and 5 warm periods over those last 500,000 years. During one of the ice ages, huge glaciers develop at the north and
paragraphs.zh: 年的历史！ 冰里面是被困了几十万年的气体。 然后，科学家可以对这些气体进行 一些化学研究，根据某些气体的比 例，他们可以知道当时的平均温 度。 从这些冰芯中，科学家们了解到， 在过去的 50 万年中，地球气候经 历了五个阶段，而过去的南极洲比 现在要冷得多。 由此他们了解到，在过去的 50 万 年里，地球经历了 5 个冰河时期和 5 个温暖期。 在其中某个冰河时代，北极和南极 出现了巨大的冰川，大量的水被锁

paragraphs.en: south poles, and so much water gets locked up in these glaciers that the ocean level drops! The world becomes much colder and drier. Then the planet warms up, the glaciers melt, the ocean levels rise and the planet becomes warm and wet once again. This is a cycle that has been repeating itself every hundred thousand years over the past five million years, or at least, the past several million years. Perhaps one of these ice ages triggered a change in the environment of the ancestor ape. Arica is close enough to the equator that it dose not develop giant glaciers.
paragraphs.zh: 在这些冰川中，以至于海平面下 降！ 世界变得越来越寒冷和干燥。 然后，地球变暖，冰川融化，海平 面上升，地球再次变得温暖潮湿。 过去的五百万年(或者说几百万 年)间，每隔几千上万年就会出现 一个这样的循环。 可能其中的一个冰川触发了古猿 生存环境的变化。 非洲距离迟到非常近，以至于在冰 河时期并没有发展处巨大的冰川。

paragraphs.en: But it does become a lot cooler and drier. This causes the forests to shrink and get replaced by grasslands. So let's imagine this ancestor ape to both humans and chimpanzees living in Africa about 5 million years ago. One group of these apes is living in the West and another group is living in the East. Both of them are living in a jungle environment. But then the climate changes, maybe from an ice-age, or shifting wind patterns, and Eastern Africa dries out. Now the Apes in the East are no longer living in a jungle. They are living in a Savannah where there is
paragraphs.zh: 但它确实变得凉爽和干燥了许多。 这导致大片森林被草原取代。 让我们想象一下，500 万年前生活 在非洲的人类和黑猩猩的共同祖 先：类人猿。 这些类人猿中有一部分生活在西 方，另一部分生活在东方。 而它们都生活在丛林中。 但随后，气候发生了变化，也许是 冰河时代来了，或者风场类型改变 了，以至于东非变得干燥了。 于是东部的类人猿不再生活在丛 林中。他们生活在热带草原上，那 里的植被是草丛和树木的混合。

paragraphs.en: a mixture of grass and trees. So, over thousands of years, these apes go from living in a place that looks like this. To living in a place that looks like this! Now you must remember that these ancient apes were small creatures. For comparison ,here is a model of an adult Australopithecus right next to an adult human woman. That meat Australopithecus were hunted. So Australopithecus had to sleep in trees at night to keep safe. But during the day, they came down from the trees to look for food. Therefore, Australopithecus probably walked dozens of miles
paragraphs.zh: 所以，几千年来，这些类人猿的生 活环境从这样(丛林)。 变成了这样(草原)！ 现在你一定还记得，这些类人猿的 体型是比较小的。 这是一个成年南方古猿的模型，它 旁边是一位成年的现代人类女性。 所以我们的祖先会被各种猛兽追 捕。 这意味着南方古猿晚上可能睡在 树上以保安全。 但在白天，他们必须从树上下来寻 找食物。 因此，南方古猿可能需要每天在草 原上行走几十英里，来寻找食物和

paragraphs.en: each day across the grasslands, looking for food and trees where they could sleep. Those Australopithecus that were better walkers were able to save energy and with the energy, they could have more, healthier, babies. And of course, their babies were carrying their genes and also became efficient walkers. Over hundreds of thousands of years, the ancester apes evolved into better walkers. So if we look at our two groups of ancestor apes. We can see different pressures from their environments after the climate changed. The apes in the West needed to be good climbers, but walking was not very important. But the apes in the East needed to
paragraphs.zh: 可以栖息的树木。 那些行走能力较好的南方古猿能 够节省体力，拥有更多、更健康的 后代。当然，因为他们的后代携带 着他们的基因，因此也变成更高效 的步行者。在数十万年的时间里， 这些物种进化成了更好的步行者。 因此，如果我们观察我们的两组祖 先类人猿，我们可以看到气候变化 后，它们分别面临着来自环境的不 同压力。 西方的类人猿需要成为攀爬专家， 对他们来说，行走并不重要。 而东部的类人猿必须同时擅长攀

paragraphs.en: be good climbers and good walkers. So the apes in the West evolved into the ancestors of Chimpanzees, while the apes in the East evolved into Australopithecus. And that is how our ancestors first began walking on two legs. So, Australopithecus represents walking: the 1st stage in our evolution. But, what is the next stage? Well, please join me next time as we answer that questions. Thank you very much.
paragraphs.zh: 爬和行走。 因此，西方的类人猿进化成了黑猩 猩的祖先，而东部的则进化成了南 方古猿。 我们的祖先就这样开始用两条腿 走路。 那么，南方古猿代表行走：这是我 们进化的第一阶段。 下一阶段又是什么呢？ 下期节目中，请大家和我一起来探 索这个问题的答案。 谢谢大家。

titleZh: 第四讲：人类发明工具居然是为了这件事

paragraphs.en: Hello! And welcome back to our lecture series on Human Origins. Today we examine Homo habilis. The 2nd ancestral species in our story. Our knowledge of Homo habilis begin in Tanzania, in a place called Olduvai Gorge. This is a picture of what Olduvai Gorge looks like. In the early 1960s, a husband-and-wife team of anthropologists named Louis and Mary Leaky were digging for bones in Olduvai Gorge. And during these expeditions they found several finger bones and
paragraphs.zh: 大家好！ 欢迎来到我们的“人类起源”系列 讲座。 今天我们要讲的是“能人”，这是 我们的故事中的第二位祖先。 我们对能人的了解始于坦桑尼亚， 一个叫做奥杜瓦伊峡谷的地方。 这张图片展示了奥杜瓦伊峡谷的 风貌。 在 20 世纪 60 年代初，一对人类学 家夫妇路易斯和玛丽·利基曾在奥 杜瓦伊峡谷挖掘骨骼化石。 在这几次野外科考中，他们发现了 几枚指骨和牙齿化石。

paragraphs.en: teeth. After careful study, the Leakys came to believe that these belonged to a new species of human ancestor which they called Homo habilis. Then 10 years later, a Kenyan man named Kamoya Kimeu made another discovery. Kamoya had started as one of Louis and Mary Leaky's workers. It turned out that he was a genius at finding ancient hominid bones, and Kamoya Kimeu quickly became the worlds’ greatest fossil hunter. In 1973, he discovered a skull of Homo habilis. Now with Kimeu's discovery, our understanding of Homo habilis became much stronger.
paragraphs.zh: 经过仔细研究，利基夫妇相信这些 化石属于一种新的人类祖先，他们 将其命名为能人。 10 年后，一个名叫卡莫亚·季默 的肯尼亚人有了新的发现。 卡莫亚一开始是利基夫妇的一个 助手。 后来他展现出惊人的找寻古人类 骨骼化石的天赋，并很成为了世界 上最优秀的化石搜寻者。 1973 年，他发现了一枚能人头骨。 如今，凭借季默的发现，我们对能 人的理解大大加深。

paragraphs.en: Habilis lived in Africa from 2.2 until 1.6 million years ago. So Habilis is about a million years more recent that Australopithecus. But Habilis was still a small creature like Australopithecus. In fact a full-grown male was only 130 centimeters tall. By comparison a male Australopithecus is 140 centimenters tall, and a male Homo sapiens is about 180 centimeters tall. But Kimeu's skull made one thing very clear. Habilis was much smarter than Australopithecus. Habilis had a brain that was about 550 cubic centimeters in side. That is not even half the size of our brains, BUT it is about 25% larger
paragraphs.zh: 能人生活在距今 220 万-160 万年 前的非洲。 所以能人要比南方古猿晚了约一 百万年。 但是和南方古猿类似，能人的体型 仍旧十分矮小。事实上，一个成年 男性也仅有 1.3 米高。 通过比较，一个男性南方古猿高 1.4 米，而一个男性智人则高约 1.8 米。 然而季默发现的头骨证实了一件 事——能人的智力远高于南方古 猿。 能人的脑容量约为 550 毫升。这连 现代人的一半都不到，但是比南方 古猿的脑容量大了 25%。

paragraphs.en: than Australopithecus. And there is another important clue to Habilis' intelligence. In Olduvai gorge, in the same places that Habilis bones were discovered, scientists have also discovered the oldest stone tools in the world. These tools are over 2 million years old. And they are very simple, basically the toolmaker took a rock, broke off part of the rock, and then used the sharp edge as a cutting blade. Scientists call these oldest stone tools 'Mode 1- Tools' because they are the earliest tools that we have found. These rocks might not look like much, but take a moment to
paragraphs.zh: 此外，关于能人的智力水平还有一 条重要的线索。 在奥杜瓦伊峡谷，能人骨骼化石出 土的地点，科学家还发现了世界上 最古老的石制工具。 这些工具有超过 200 万年的历史。 工具本身非常简单，基本就是将一 块石头破开，然后用尖锐的边缘进 行切割。 科学家将这些最古老的石制工具 命名为“初代工具”，因为它们是 我们能找到的最早的工具。 这些石头可能看起来不太像工具， 但是请仔细端详一番。

paragraphs.en: appreciate what you are looking at. Habilis represents the moment when our ancestors went from being just another animal, to a creature that could make tools and shape the world.These stones represent the very beginning of human technology. To understand why Habilis would have found such tool useful, lets think about what happens in Africa when a big predator like a lion kills a big animal like a zebra or wildebeest. First the lions eat the zebra. Now for a small weak creature like Habilis, lions are very dangerous animals. You would want to stay far away from them. After the lions have eaten their fill, then the hyenas move in and eat
paragraphs.zh: 能人的出现代表我们的祖先脱离 动物性，成为了会制造工具并塑造 世界的物种。这些石头代表着人类 技术的起源。 要理解这些工具对能人的作用，我 们可以想一想，在非洲大草原上， 狮子之类的大型捕食者猎杀斑马 或角马这种大型动物的情形。 首先狮子会把斑马吃掉。而对于能 人这样弱小的物种，狮子是极其危 险的动物。你得躲得它们远远的。 狮子吃饱之后，鬣狗就会来吃剩下 的猎物。

paragraphs.en: what's left. Hyenas are also very dangerous for a small, weak creature like Habilis, so they would want to stay away. And after the Hyenas the vultures come in and polish off everything that's left. Vultures are big, nasty, birds. Habilis might be able to drive them away from a kill, but it would be difficult. So by the time the animal carcass is safe to come near, all of the meat has been eaten. Except there is one last meal that the lions, hyenas and vultures cannot get to. Inside the bones of the animal is the marrow.
paragraphs.zh: 对弱小的能人来说，鬣狗也是非常 危险的，所以他们也得躲远一点。 鬣狗吃完之后，秃鹫又会来打扫残 局。 秃鹫是大型猛禽。能人或许可以把 它们从猎物尸体旁赶跑，但那也要 费一番力气。 所以，等到动物的残骸可以安全接 近时，肉都已经被吃光了。 但是还有最后仅剩的一种食物，是 狮子、鬣狗，和秃鹫都无法染指的。 动物的骨骼中是骨髓。

paragraphs.en: Stone tools are perfect for breaking the bones open to reveal the marrow inside. Habilis probably first used stones to crack the bones open. And then they would feast on the marrow inside. It might not seem very delicious to us modern humans, but in the African wilderness, a meal like this could mean the difference between survival and death. And it marks another big change in our history. Most of the Great Apes, including Australopithecus, were mainly vegetarians. But with Habilis, or ancestors began to eat meat.
paragraphs.zh: 石制工具非常适合敲开骨头，获取 里面的骨髓。 能人很可能先用石头将骨头敲开。 然后他们会食用里面的骨髓。 对我们现代人来说，骨髓看起来不 是很美味，但是在非洲的荒野中， 这样一餐可是关乎生存的。 这还标志着人类历史上的又一次 重大变革。 大部分类人猿，包括南方古猿，都 基本上是素食者。 但是从能人开始，我们的祖先开始 食肉了。

paragraphs.en: There is other evidence of Habilis' meat eating besides cracked bones. In the same place where scientists find Habilis fossils and Mode 1 Stone tools, they also find the bones of big animals like wildebeests and zebras. But when they look closely at these bones from the wildebeasts and the zebras, they notice something. There are cut marks in the bone that were made by stone tools. Here is another photograph, showing you these cut marks. This means that Habilis was also using these stone tools to butcher dead animals and cut the meat off the bones. Perhaps if a large group of Habilis found a dead animal before anyone
paragraphs.zh: 除了破碎的骨头外，还有其他证据 可以证明能人的肉食性。 在科学家发现能人化石和“初代工 具”的同一地点，他们还发现了角 马和斑马一类的大型动物骨骼。 但是当他们近距离观察这些角马 和斑马的骨骼时，他们注意到一些 东西。 骨头上有被石制工具切割过的痕 迹。 这是另一张展示这些切割痕迹的 图片。 这意味着能人还用这些石制工具 分解动物尸体，并将肉从骨头上割 下来。 也许，如果一大群能人率先发现了 一头死去的动物，他们就可以吓退

paragraphs.en: else, they could scare off other scavengers and then use their tools to cut up the dead animal. If they found a big dead animal like an elephant, they could eat for days off of the meat from something like that. And of course, there is no way for us humans to cut open something as thick as elephant skin without tools. Our teeth and fingernails are just not up the job. So Habilis began using stone tools, and that allowed him to start scavenging meat. But why is meat-eating so important to our evolution？ Well it is important because meat is much more nutrient-dense than plants are.
paragraphs.zh: 其他食腐动物，并用工具分割尸 体。 要是他们发现了像大象这种大型 动物的尸体，他们能吃上好几天。 当然，我们人类不借助工具的话， 是不可能割开厚厚的大象皮肤的。 我们的牙齿和指甲可做不来这种 事。 所以能人开始使用石制工具，这让 他们得以食腐肉维持生存。 但是为什么食肉对我们的进化如 此重要呢？ 这很重要，因为肉类比植物要更富 含营养。

paragraphs.en: If you want to eat the daily recommended amount of iron, you only need to eat 300 grams of cow liver. But you would need to eat 2 and a half kilograms of spinach to get the same amount. You can see this is our bodies. Chimpanzees spend their whole days eating plants, so they have big powerful jaws and teeth, that are good at chewing fiber. As we started to eat meat, and started to cook, our jaws shrank, because we didn't need to do as much chewing. Look at the difference between a chimpanzee jaw and a human jaw. That shows you the difference in our diets right there.
paragraphs.zh: 如果你想摄入每日所需数量的铁 元素，你只需食用 300 克牛肝。 但是你需要食用 2.5 千克菠菜才 能摄入等量的铁。 你可以看到这是我们的身体。 黑猩猩终日吃素，所以他们有着宽 大的下颚和牙齿，适于咀嚼植物纤 维。 当我们开始吃肉，并开始烹调食物 时，我们的下颚就开始萎缩，因为 我们不需要进行那么多的咀嚼了。 请看黑猩猩和人类下颚的区别。这 显示出人跟猩猩有着不同的饮食 习性。

paragraphs.en: Another thing that happened is our guts got smaller. If you are eating huge amounts of plants every day, you need a big gut and a long intestine to digest it all. But if you start eating meat, then you don't need a gut that is quite as big anymore. So as we became meat eaters our guts shrank. The picture on the left shows you an Australopithecus gut, while the one on the right shows you a Homo erectus gut, and hominid who lived 1.5 million years after Australopithecus. You can see how the gut shrank during that time. And as ours guts shrank, our brains got bigger. This is not a coincidence. As the
paragraphs.zh: 食肉的另一个影响就是我们的肠 胃会变小。 如果你每天食用巨量的植物，你就 需要一个巨大的胃和很长的肠道 来消化这些植物。 但如果你开始吃肉了，那么你就不 需要那么大的肠胃了。 所以当我们开始食肉后，我们的肠 胃就萎缩了。 图片左侧展示的是南方古猿的肠 胃，右侧是直立人的肠胃，这是比 南方古猿晚 150 万年的原始人类。 你可以看出在这一时期人类的肠 胃缩小了多少。 在我们的肠胃缩小的同时，我们的 大脑变得更大了。 这不是什么巧合。由于身体不再需

paragraphs.en: body needed to spend less energy maintaining a big gut, it had more energy for supporting a big brain. And it needed that energy because a big brain uses a lot of energy. Our brains are only 2% of our body weight but they use 20% of our energy. In summary, meat eating was necessary for our brains to get bigger. But, I would also like to point out something. Although Homo habilis ate meat, we think they were scavengers NOT hunters. A hunter is a creature that kills for itself, like a lion. A scavenger is a creature that eats
paragraphs.zh: 要那么多能量来维持巨大的肠胃， 就有更多的能量可以供给大脑。 这些能量是必要的，因为更大的大 脑会消耗很多能量。 我们的大脑仅占身体重量的 2%。 却要消耗 20%的能量。 总之，大脑变大，食肉是必不可少 的。 但是，我想指出一点。 尽管能人是食肉的，但我们仍将他 们看作食腐者，而不是捕食者。 捕食者是有捕猎能力的生物，比如 狮子。 食腐者是食用其他动物的猎物的

Even though Habilis was starting to eat meat, that did not mean it was on top of the food chain. We have seen tooth marks on the skull of Habilis or through the foot bones. This is strong evidence that Habilis was hunted and eaten by big predators like Dinofelis. Habilis might have been learning to eat meat, but he was still food for many other creatures. It was not until the next stage in our evolution that we climbed our way to the top of the food chain.
paragraphs.zh: 生物。 能人更像是秃鹫，而不是狮子。 还有一件事需要知道。尽管能人已 经开始吃肉了，这并不意味着他们 已经站上了食物链顶端。 我们在能人的头骨和脚骨上发现 了牙齿咬过的痕迹。 这是一个有力的证据，证明能人会 被恐猫之类的大型捕食者猎杀。 能人或许已经学会了吃肉，但是他 们仍然是很多其他生物的食物。 直到人类进化的下一阶段，我们才 跻身于食物链顶端。

paragraphs.en: This is when we discovered our secret weapons. And that’s our topic for next time.Thank you, everyone.
paragraphs.zh: 那就是当人类发现了秘密武器之 时。 不过那是我们下节课要讲的内容 了，谢谢。

titleZh: 第五讲：直立人时代：火为人类带来了什么？

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our lecture series on World History. Today we are going to discuss the next ancestor in our history: Homo erectus. The first fossil of Homo erectus ever discovered was found by a man named Eugene DuBoi while he was digging for fossils in Indonesia. These fossils were found in the 1890s on the island of Java in the Indonesian archipelago. Eugene DuBois found the top of a skull and a leg bone. The bones did not seem to match those of modern humans or any living ape species. So DuBois
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列讲 座。 今天我们要讨论的是我们历史上 的下一代祖先：直立人。 一个名叫尤金·杜布瓦的人在印度 尼西亚挖掘化石时，发现了最早的 直立人化石。 这些化石是在印度尼西亚群岛的 爪哇群岛发现的。 尤金·杜布瓦发现了一块头盖骨和 一根股骨。 这些骨头似乎与现代人类或任何 现存的猿类物种都不匹配。所以杜 布瓦认为他发现的是人类的祖先。

paragraphs.en: thought that he had discovered a human ancestor. He claimed that this animal was a species in-between humans and apes. This creature became known as 'Java-man'. But many other scientists were skeptical. However, 30 years later in 1921, more bones from this creature were discovered just outside of Beijing at a site called Zhou Kou Dian. The paleontologists Johan Anderson and Walter Granger searched zhoukoudian for 7 years and found several teeth, which they believed belonged to an ancient human ancestor. From 1929 until the beginning of
paragraphs.zh: 他主张这种动物是介于人类和猿 类之间的一个物种。 这种生物被称为“爪哇猿人” 但许多其他科学家对此表示怀疑。 然而，30 年后的 1921 年，在北京 郊外一个名为周口店的地方，发现 了更多的这种骨头。 古生物学家约翰·安德森和沃尔 特·格兰杰在周口店进行了 7 年的 搜寻，发现了几颗牙齿，他们认为 这些牙齿属于远古人类祖先。 从 1929 年到二战开始，该遗址的

paragraphs.en: World War 2, the excavation of the site was led by 3 Chinese paleontologists: Yang Zhongjian, Pei Wenzhong, and Jia Lanpo. These men discovered the skulls of several individuals. Which led to a huge advance in our understanding. The name given to these creatures was 'Peking Man.' Several other fossils from this creature were discovered in the following decades. But then in 1984 an astonishing find was made in Kenya, again by Kamoya Kimeu, who is probably the greatest fossil hunter of all time. Kamoya was searching in northwest Kenya near lake Turkana. Here is a picture of that Lake.
paragraphs.zh: 发掘工作由 3 名中国古生物学家 主导：杨钟健、裴文忠和贾兰坡。 这三位古生物学家发现了多枚头 骨，这使得我们对远古人类的认知 有了重大进展。 他们给这些古生物取名为“北京 人”。 接下来的几十年里，学者们陆续又 发现了这种生物的化石。 但随后，在 1984 年，卡莫亚·季 默在肯尼亚又有了重大发现。他或 许称得上是人类历史上最伟大的 化石猎人。 卡莫亚在肯尼亚西北部的图尔卡 纳湖附近进行搜索。 这是图尔卡纳湖的照片。

paragraphs.en: There, Kimoya Kimeu found not just a skull, but an entire skeleton! This discovery was given the nickname 'Turkana Boy' and it is the most complete hominid skeleton ever discovered. All of these discoveries, Java man, Peking man, Turkana boy, all of them are examples of a species we now call Homo erectus. For me at least, Homo erectus feels like the moment that we stopped being apes, and we became people. Homo erectus lived about 300,000 years after Homo habilis. Homo erectus had a brain that was about 1000 cubic centimeters in volume. That is only about 70% the size of our brains. But it is almost double
paragraphs.zh: 卡莫亚不仅发现了一枚头骨，还发 现了一具完整的骨骼！ 这一发现被称为“图尔卡纳男孩”， 是迄今为止发现的最完整的古人 类骨骼。 所有这些发现，爪哇猿人、北京人、 图尔卡纳男孩，都是我们现在称之 为直立人的例子。 至少于我而言，进入到直立人时 代，就意味着我们从猿猴变成了 人。 直立人约生活在能人的 30 万年 后。 直立人的脑容量约有 1000 毫升。 这仅仅是人类大脑的 70%，但几乎 是能人的两倍。因此，直立人是一

paragraphs.en: the size of Homo habilis brains. So it was a very intelligent creature. Homo erectus was big too. There were about as big as we are. That made them much larger than Australopithecus or Homo habilis. Homo erectus also shows another big advance in tool-making. As we mentioned last time, the first stone tools were probably made by Homo habilis about 2 million years ago. These were rocks with one edge broken-off to make a sharp blade. Paleontologists call these Mode 1 tools. With the arrival of Homo erectus, we see an advance in stone-tool technology.
paragraphs.zh: 种非常聪明的生物。 直立人体型也很大，几乎和现代人 类差不多。 也就是说，他们比南方古猿和能人 都要大。 直立人在工具制造方面也有了巨 大进步。 正如我们上节课提到的，第一个石 器工具大概是在 200 万年前由能 人制造的。他们将断裂的岩石边缘 制造成锋利的刀刃。 古生物学家将其称为初代工具。 随着直立人的到来，我们看到了石 器技术的进步。

paragraphs.en: Beginning about 1.7 million years ago, we start finding stones that have been carefully shaped into hand axes. Paleontologists call these Mode 2 tools. And most importantly of all. Many scientists think that Homo erectus learned how to control fire. Fire has many important benefits. The first is protection. All the other animals are afraid of fire. With the control of fire Homo erectus was able to scare off the big predators and made it safe for them to live on the ground at night-time. Fire also provides another big benefit: cooking. We think that Homo erectus may have been the first human species
paragraphs.zh: 大约从 170 万年前开始，我们开始 寻找那些被精心制作成手斧的石 头。 古生物学家将其称为二代工具。 最重要的是，许多科学家认为直立 人学会了用火。 火有许多重要的好处。首先是保护 作用。 所有的动物都怕火。有了火之后， 直立人能够吓跑大型捕食者，使他 们在夜间能够安全地在地面上生 活。 火还提供了另一大好处：烹饪。 我们认为直立人可能是第一个能 够烹饪食物的人类物种。

paragraphs.en: to cook their food. This makes food safer to eat, and easier to digest. Which allowed their growing brains to get even more energy from their diet. And finally, the fire led to increased social life. At the end of the day, we think that families of Homo erectus would gather around a fire, and enjoy the safety and warmth it provided while they waited for the food to cook. Well,what you are going do when you are sitting around the fire,waiting with your family and friends?You are going to theory that the cintrol of fire was also the key to the development of language. Certainly there is something
paragraphs.zh: 这使得食物更安全，更容易消化， 从而使大脑能够从饮食中获得更 多的能量。 最后，火还导致了社会生活的增 加。 在一天结束的时候，我们认为直立 人的家庭会聚集在篝火旁，一边等 待食物烤熟，一边享受篝火带来的 安全和温暖。 当你和家人还有朋友一起坐在簧 火旁时，你会做什么呢？你会开始 社交，而且很有可能你们会开始交 谈。有一种理论认为，使用火对于 人类语言的发展有着至关重要的 作用。 当然，围坐在篝火旁的习俗被永久

paragraphs.en: timeless about sitting around a fire. If you have ever enjoyed the experience of sitting around a campfire at night with friends, then you are participating in a human tradition that is 1.5 million years old! We cannot be sure, but many scientists believe that Homo erectus marks the point where we humans first developed basic spoken language. And really,I think it’s language that makes us human more than anything else,because language allows us to work together in much more complicated ways.Language allow us to socialize.Language allow us to teach each other. And language also makes living with other human beings mush more complicated.And understanding the
paragraphs.zh: 地传承了下来。如果你曾经享受过 夜晚和朋友们一起围坐在篝火旁， 那么你就是在参与一项拥有长达 150 万年历史的人类传统！ 我们无法确定，但许多科学家认 为，直立人标志着我们人类第一次 发展出了基本的口语。 我真心认为语言才是人之所以为 人的最大原因，因为语言让我们已 更复杂的方式相互协作。它让人类 的社交有了可能，它帮助我们互相 学习，同时，语言也让人类的生活 变得更加复杂。思考和理解跟我们 共同生活的其他人类可能也是我 们大脑变得越来越大的原因之一： 为了弄明白我们的同类。

paragraphs.en: fellow human beings we were living with was probably another reason why our brains got bigger,so that we could figure each other out Now this brings up a question. Just how did Homo erectus catch that meat that they would cook around the fire. How did they catch their prey with just stone hand-axes and wooden spears. That is the question we will answer in part II of our lecture on Homo erectus. Thank you very much.
paragraphs.zh: 这就引出了一个问题。 那就是：直立人是如何捕获他们用 火炙烤的食材的呢？ 他们是如何用石头手斧和木矛抓 住猎物的？ 相关问题我们将在直立人第二部 分进行讨论，谢谢。

titleZh: 第六讲：万万没想到，没有武器加持的原始直立人是这样捕猎的！

paragraphs.en: Hello, and welcome back to part II of our lecture on Homo erectus. Last time we ended with a question: how did Homo erectus catch the meat that they would cook around the fire? How did they catch their prey with just stone hand-axes and wooden spears? The answer:Homo erectus, like us was a very good long distance runner. We humans are not fast runners, but we can run for very long distances. And there are a number of adaptations in our human bodies that show that running is important in our evolution. This is an illustration that compares
paragraphs.zh: 欢迎回到直立人的第二部分，上一 讲我们提出了这样的问题：直立人 是如何捕获他们用火炙烤的食材 的呢？他们又是如何用石斧和木 矛抓住猎物的呢？ 答案是：直立人，和我们人类一样， 都非常善于长跑。 我们人类不是高速的奔跑者，但我 们可以跑动非常长的距离。 我们人体中的一系列适应性变化 表明，跑步在我们的进化中占有非 常重要的地位。 这张图解比较了直立人和南方古

paragraphs.en: the differences in Homo erectus and Australopithecus skeletons. Today we will look at some of these many differences in more details. A lot of them,a lot of the change between Australopithecus and Homo erectus has to do with the development of running ability. The first is an adaptation that Australopithecus also had. Our human big toes point forwards. They are not opposable like the big toes of a chimpanzee. A chimpanzee's big toe is good for grabbing and climbing. And human big toe is good for pushing off with when we run or walk. The second big adaptation is our big butts.
paragraphs.zh: 猿的骨骼差异。 今天我们将更详细地讨论其中的 一些不同。 很多南方古猿和直立人的差别都 跟奔跑能力有关。 首先，我要讲的是一种南方古猿也 具有的适应性变化。 我们人类的大脚趾指向前方，而黑 猩猩的大脚趾指向其它脚趾。 黑猩猩的大脚趾更适合抓取和攀 爬，而人类的大脚趾则能在我们行 走或奔跑时更好地将身体推离地 面。 第二大适应性变化是我们的大屁 股。

paragraphs.en: What I mean by this is the shape of the Gluteus Maximus muscle. On humans the Gluteus Maximus muscle is very large. This is the muscle that gives our rear-ends their shape. You can see this most clearly when you compare our Gluteus Maximus to those of Chimpanzees. Our butt muscles are much bigger and more prominent than theirs. And this is because you need a big strong butt in order to stay upright when you run. Walking does not require big butt muscles, but running does. The third big adaptation is sweating. This is an adaptation that other big mammals do not have.
paragraphs.zh: 我指的是臀大肌的形状。 人类的臀大肌非常大。 正是臀大肌塑造了我们的臀部轮 廓。 如果把我们的臀大肌和黑猩猩相 比较，你可以更清楚地看到这一 点。 我们的臀部肌肉比它们大得多，也 更突出。 这是因为当你奔跑时，你需要强壮 的臀部来保持直立。 行走不需要强壮的臀部肌肉，但跑 步需要。 第三大适应性变化是流汗。这是其 他大型哺乳动物没有的适应性变 化。

paragraphs.en: Lions and dogs need to lose heat by panting or by staying in the shade. In the mid-day summer heat, lions don't move that much. They do most of their hunting at night. But sweating allows us humans to stay cool when it is very hot outside. We humans also have a special ligament that the great apes do not have. This ligament is called the Nuchal Ligament and it helps our head stay upright and stable while we run. We know that Homo erectus had this muscle too, because we can see the place in the skull where the ligament attached to the bone. And so all of these adaptation have led to a theory that Homo erectus
paragraphs.zh: 狮子和狗需要通过喘气或待在阴 凉处来散热。 在烈日酷暑的中午，狮子一般很少 活动。它们大部分的狩猎活动都是 在晚上进行的。 但是，当室外非常热的时候，出汗 可以让我们人类保持凉爽。 我们人类还有一种特殊的韧带，这 是类人猿所不具备的。 这个韧带叫做颈韧带，当我们奔跑 时，它能够帮助我们的头部保持直 立和稳定。 我们知道直立人也有这种肌肉，因 为我们可以看到头骨中韧带附着 在骨头上的位置。 所有这些适应性变化都指向一种 理论：直立人通过“持续性狩猎”

paragraphs.en: got its meat through something called the 'Persistence Hunt'. Homo erectus, was not a fast runner, but like us, it was an excellent long-distance runner. In hot weather Homo erectus would chase it prey for many hours, until the animal had a heat stroke and collapsed. Even today there is a group of people called the Khoi-San bushmen who will use this hunting technique. The Khoi-San live in South Africa, and the man on the right is a member of that tribe. First the Khoi-San hunters will go out looking for animal tracks. Once they have found some animal tracks, they determine the general
paragraphs.zh: 来获取肉食。 直立人，并不擅长短程速跑，但却 和人类一样，非常擅长长跑。 在炎热的天气里，直立人能够连续 数小时追逐猎物，直到它中暑晕 倒。 直到今天，科伊桑-布须曼人仍然 使用这种技巧来捕猎。 科伊桑人生活在南非，图片右侧的 人是该部落的一员。 首先，科伊桑猎人外出寻找动物的 踪迹。 一旦发现足迹，他们就确定了猎物 的大致方向。

paragraphs.en: direction of their prey. Then the chase begins. They will pursue these animals and run in a hot mid-day African sun for as long as eight to twelve hours. Imagine running for eight hours in the hot sun! But these Khoi-San hunters do it all the time. Then after a long pursuit the animal finally collapsed from heat-stroke and exhaustion, and the hunters finish it off with a club or spear. Other animals may be faster, but we humans are some of the best long distance runners on planet earth, especially in hot weather. Remember, we can sweat and carry water, other mammals cannot. In addition to running ability, the persistence hunt requires tracking
paragraphs.zh: 然后，追逐开始了。他们会在非洲 炎热的太阳下追逐猎物长达八到 十二个小时。 想象一下，在烈日下连续奔跑八小 时！而这对科伊桑的猎人们来说， 只是生活常态。 经过长时间的追击，猎物终于因中 暑和精疲力竭而倒下了，猎人们用 棍子或长矛把它干掉了。 其它动物可能跑得更快，但我们人 类是地球上最擅长长跑的动物之 一，尤其是在炎热的天气里。 记住，我们可以流汗，还可以随身 携带水源。其它哺乳动物却不能。 除了奔跑能力之外，持续性狩猎还 对搜索能力有所要求。

paragraphs.en: ability. This suggests that Homo erectus could think abstractly. A Homo erectus hunter could look at tracks in the sand and do something unique in the history of life on earth. Homo erectus could realize that these marks in the earth were made by an animal, and that animal would be nearby. So let's put it all together. Homo erectus was the first species on earth to master a group of very human abilities. They had fire, stone tools,basic language, abstract thought, team-work and runing ability. Since we have discovered Homo erectus fossils in Africa, southern
paragraphs.zh: 这表明直立人可以抽象地思考。 直立人可以观察沙子中的踪迹，做 一些地球生命史上独一无二的事 情。 直立人能够意识到这些土地上的 痕迹是由动物造成的，而这个动物 很有可能就在附近。 让我们把这些事实拼到一起来看。 直立人是地球上第一种掌握了一 系列人类技能的物种。 他们又会、石器、基本语言、抽象 思维、团队合作和跑步能力。 由于我们在非洲、南亚和欧洲都发 现了直立人的化石，我们认为直立

paragraphs.en: Asia and Europe, we think this was the first human ancestor to migrate out of Africa. This map shows you the parts of the world where we think Homo erectus lived. The red dots show you where we have discovered Homo erectus bones. Yet despite Homo erectus' remarkable achievements, there are still ways that their minds fell short of ours. We can see this most clearly in their tools. On the left,is a Homo erectus hand axe from 1.7 million years ago. And on the right, is a Homo erectus hand axe from 500,000 years ago. For over 1 million years, there was
paragraphs.zh: 人就是第一代走出非洲，迈向世界 的人类祖先。 这张地图展示了我们推测直立人 生活的地方。 红点显示了我们发现直立人骨头 的位置。 然而，尽管直立人取得了令人瞩目 的成就，但他们的思想仍然落后于 我们。 我们可以从他们的工具中看到这 一点。 左图是 170 万年前的直立人使用 的手斧。 右图是 50 万年前的直立人使用的 手斧。 在 100 多万年的时间里，直立人的

paragraphs.en: no advance in the technology of Homo erectus. Imagine if our technology made no improvements in 1 million years! For this reason, many paleontologists think that Homo erectus made stone tools in the same way a bird builds a nest. It was more of an instinctive behavior than one that involved conscious thought and study. But of course, change continued to happen in our brains and in our bodies during those one million years. Please join me next time as we discuss the next steps in Human evolution. Homo Heidelbergensis and the Neanderthals.
paragraphs.zh: 技术并没有取得进步。 想象一下，如果我们的技术在 100 万年里没有任何改进！ 出于这个原因，许多古生物学家认 为直立人制造石器的方式与鸟类 筑巢的方式是一样的。 它更多地是一种本能的过程，而不 是有意识的思考和研究。 当然，在这一百万年里，我们的大 脑和身体都在持续发生变化。 下节课，让我们共同讨论人类进化 的下一个阶段。 海德堡人和尼安德特人。

titleZh: 第七讲：尼安德特人比我们想象中更像人类

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our lecture series on Human Origins. Today we will discuss the 4th step in our evolution: Homo Heidelbergensis and the Neanderthals. Starting about 600,000 years ago a new human ancestor emerged. These creatures are known as Homo Heidelbergensis. They had brains that were almost as big as ours, and bodies that were the same size. This species of Human was first discovered in Germany in the year 1908 by a quarry worker named Daniel Hartman. He had been trained in how to
paragraphs.zh: 大家好！欢迎回到我们的“人类 起源”系列讲座。 今天我们要讨论的是人类进化中 的第四个阶段：海德堡人和尼安 德特人。 大约 60 万年前，一种新的人类祖 先出现了。 他们被称为海德堡人。 他们的脑容量和体型都与我们现 代人相差无几。 这个人种最先由一位名叫丹尼 尔·哈特曼的采石工人于 1908 年 在德国发现。 当地一位名叫奥图·萧顿萨克的

paragraphs.en: identify fossils by a local professor named Otto Schoetensack. Professor Schoetensack identified the fossil as a human ancestor and gave it the name ： Homo Heidelbergensis. This was in honor of the city of Heidelberg which was nearby towards the fossil was found. Homo Heidelbergensis was definitely a hunter. In fact we think they hunted very big animals like elephants. Although they may not have spread as far as Homo erectus. We think their range was limited to Europe and Africa, but who knows perhaps we will find their fossils in Asia too one day.
paragraphs.zh: 教授训练过他识别化石的能力。 萧顿萨克教授认定这是一枚人类 祖先的化石，并将这个人种命名 为海德堡人。 这个名字来源于化石发现地点附 近的海德堡市。 可以肯定，海德堡人是捕猎者。 事实上，我们相信他们能捕猎大 象一类的超大型动物。 尽管他们并不像直立人那样分布 广泛。 我们目前认为他们的分布范围仅 限于欧洲及非洲地区，但是或许 有朝一日，在亚洲也可能会发现 他们的化石。

paragraphs.en: About 400,000 years ago, a group of Heidelbergensis living in Europe developed slightly bigger brains and evolved into Homo neanderthalensis, or 'Neanderthals' for short. The Neanderthals existed from 400,000 to 40,000 years ago. And they had brains that were the same size as ours, although those brains may have been organized differently. Neanderthals were also discovered in Germany. The first bones were found in the Neander valley in 1856. The bones were originally discovered in a limestone quarry that probably looked a lot like this one. So, these people are named after the
paragraphs.zh: 大约 40 万年前，一群生活在欧洲 的海德堡人的大脑有了略微的增 长，并进化为尼安德特人。 尼安德特人存在于 40 万年至 4 万 年前。他们的大脑体积与现代人 几乎相同，尽管二者的大脑可能 构造不同。 尼安德特人也发现于德国。最早 的一批化石在 1856 年出土于尼安 德山谷。 这些骨骼化石最初是在一座类似 这样的采石场中发现的。 这一人种正是以这座山谷命名

paragraphs.en: Neander valley. Here is a picture of the stream located in that valley. Here are the original bones. As you can see they discovered the top of a skull, some arm and leg bones, and a few other pieces. The original site was destroyed by the quarry mine, but today there is a museum where they were discovered. At the museum you can see this artists interpretation of what the owner of these bones may have looked like. The bones were identified by two German scientists: Johann Carl Fuhlrott and Herman Schaafhausen. They published their discoveries in 1858.
paragraphs.zh: 的。 这是一条谷中的小溪。 这就是那些骨骼化石。你可以看 到，他们发现了一枚头盖骨、一 些四肢骨骼，还有一些其它骨骼。 由于矿石开采，化石出土地点已 遭到破坏，但是如今那里建起了 一座博物馆。 馆内你可以看到这幅图片，这是 艺术家推测中这些骨头的主人的 相貌。 这些骨骼化石是被两名德国科学 家鉴别出来的：约翰·卡尔·富 尔罗特与赫尔曼·沙夫豪森。他 们于 1858 年发表了他们的研究发 现。

paragraphs.en: But remember, this was 1858. More than 30 years before the discovery of Java man or any other ancient human ancestor. In the 1850s people didn't really know what to make of these bones. Some people thought they were from an ape, others thought they were from a bear, or maybe a deformed human. But then the next year in 1859, Charles Darwin published his famous book 'The Origin of the Species' which suggested that humans evolved from apes, and laid out his general theory of evolution. Perhaps these weird bones belonged to some ape-man ancestor of ours? In 1864, William King was the first to suggest that these bones belonged to a different species of
paragraphs.zh: 但是请记住，这是在 1858 年。要 到 30 年之后爪哇猿人或其他人类 祖先的化石才出土。 在 19 世纪 50 年代，人们甚至还 不知道这些骨骼到底属于什么物 种。有人认为是猿类，还有人认 为是熊类，或者也可能是畸形人 类的。 但是在次年，即 1859 年，查尔 斯·达尔文发表了他的名著《物 种起源》，提出人类是由猿猴进 化而来。 没准这些奇怪的骨头就是属于我 们的某种人猿祖先呢？ 在 1864 年，威廉·金最先提出这 些骨骼属于一个不同的人种。

paragraphs.en: human. He suggested calling this species Homo neanderthalensis. Neanderthal eventually became the name. But in 1866 Ernest Haekel suggested calling them Homo stupidus instead. Fortunately, this name was not chosen. In the early 1900s the leading scientists Arthur Keith and Marcelin Boule concluded that Neanderthals were too primitive to be a human ancestor. They thought that the creature was some sort of stupid ape-like creature. This influenced early depictions of
paragraphs.zh: 他建议将这个人种命名为尼安德 特人。 这个名称最终被敲定。 然而在 1866 年，欧内斯特·海克 尔建议称他们为“愚人”。 幸运的是，这个名字没有被采纳。 在 20 世纪初，两位科学带头人亚 瑟·凯斯和马瑟兰·布勒下定结 论，尼安德特人过于原始，不可 能是人类的祖先。 他们认为这种生物只是某种低智 能的猿类。 这一观点导致人们在过去将尼安

paragraphs.en: Neanderthals as primitive ape men. In fact have you ever seen the cartoons that we have of cave men and thick-browed, stupid, kind of knuckle-dragging, brutes come from this early conception of Neanderthals, I’m sure you’ve seen these in cartoons. However, these early depictions of Neanderthals probably tell us more about the prejudice of these early scientists than they do about Neanderthals themselves. Fortunately, in the 1930s, several scientists led by George Simpson, Ernst Mayer and Theodosius Dobzhnasky pushed for a re-interpretation of these creatures. They suggested that Neanderthals were full members of Homo sapiens just like us.
paragraphs.zh: 德特人描绘成原始的人猿。 实际上，大部分动画片中出现的 眉毛粗重、手指后翻的野蛮穴居 人形象，都是来源于这种对尼安 德特人的早期观点。 但是，这些早期的描绘更多地只 能显示出早期科学家存在的偏 见，而不能为我们提供太多关于 尼安德特人的信息。 幸运的是，在 20 世纪 30 年代， 由乔治·辛普森、恩斯特·梅耶， 和迪奥多西·多伯兹纳斯基主导 的一群科学家促成了一场对尼安 德特人的再次研究。 他们提出尼安德特人完全就是智 人家族的一员，同我们现代人一 样。

paragraphs.en: And they were right. Gradually this began to change the popular conception of Neanderthals. But as you can see from this picture made in the 1950s and 60s, they were still depicted as primitive hairy brutes. But then in the late 1950s and early 1960s a series of discoveries in Iraq changed our understanding of Neanderthals in a big way. In 1957, the Paleontologist Ralph Solecki discovered several Neanderthal bones in a cave in Northern Iraq. The cave was located in a place called the Shanidar Valley. Here is what the Shanidar valley looks like.
paragraphs.zh: 他们是对的。 关于尼安德特人的主流观点自此 开始逐渐改变。 但是请看这张绘于 20 世纪五六十 年代的图片，他们仍然被画成了 多毛的原始人。 然而在 50 年代末、60 年代初，在 伊拉克的一系列发现极大地改变 了我们对尼安德特人的看法。 1957 年，古生物学家拉尔夫·索 列基在伊拉克北部的一个洞穴中 发现了几枚尼安德特人的骨骼化 石。 这个洞穴位于一处名为沙尼达尔 山谷的地带。 这就是沙尼达尔山谷的风貌。

paragraphs.en: And this is the cave that Ralph Solecki investigated. Over the years he dug up 9 Neanderthal skeletons. But to me the most interesting is the first skeleton: Shanidar 1. The skeleton belonged to a man who had died in his mid 40s. The skull over his left eye showed evidence of a crushing blow during childhood. The bones had healed, but the injury probably left the poor man blind in his left eye, and partially paralyzed on his right side. The mans right arm was also deformed. Perhaps the paralysis had caused it to stop growing. For comparison, here is the arm belonging to Shanidar 1, and here is a healthy Neanderthal arm.
paragraphs.zh: 而这就是拉尔夫·索列基调查过 的山洞。 在几年的研究中，他共挖掘出 9 具尼安德特人的遗骨。 但对我来说最有趣的是第一具遗 骨：沙尼达尔 1 号。 这具骨骼属于一名在四十多岁死 亡的男性。他的头骨左眼上方有 童年遭受过重击的痕迹。骨伤已 经愈合了，然而这次受伤导致这 个可怜的男子左眼失明，并且身 体右侧瘫痪。 这名男子的右臂也是畸形的。或 许是由于瘫痪导致其停止了发 育。 作为比较，这是沙尼达尔 1 号的 臂骨，而这是一根健康的尼安德 特人的臂骨。

paragraphs.en: You can see the difference for yourself. Furthermore so, this poor man also had bony growth over his ear sockets. Probably the result of series injuries inflicted on the side of his head. This is a model of what the Shanidar 1 man may have looked like. This man was blind in his left eye, the right side of his body was partially paralyzed; his right arm was withered, he had a fractured foot and he was deaf! And yet none of these injuries are what killed him. He lived for years after getting all of these injuries, and he died in his mid-40s, which is very old for the stone age.
paragraphs.zh: 你可以看到二者的区别。 除此之外，这个可怜的男人的耳 窝处还有骨质增生。或许这是他 头部一侧多次受伤造成的。 这是沙尼达尔 1 号的相貌复原模 型。 这名男子左眼失明、身体右侧瘫 痪、右臂萎缩，并且他还脚部骨 折、耳朵失聪。 但这些伤势都不是导致他死亡的 原因。他受了这些伤之后还活了 很多年，直到 40 岁中旬才死去， 这在石器时代已经算是长寿了。

paragraphs.en: It is impossible that this man could have taken care of himself with all of these injuries. So there is only one conclusion: this man was loved by his friends and family, who fed him and took care of him and kept him alive when he could no longer care himself. We have also found strong evidence that the Neanderthals buried their dead. In short, these were a people who loved their family and friends. They took care of their sick and buried their dead. These discoveries began to change the way we thought about the Neanderthals. So please join me in part II of our lesson on the Neanderthals. We will look at how our conception of these
paragraphs.zh: 在遭受了这些伤病的情况下，这 名男子不可能有自理能力。所以 只有一种结果：这个男人得到了 朋友和家人的关爱，他们照顾着 他的生活。 我们还发现了有力的证据，证明 尼安德特人会埋葬死者。 简言之，他们有着亲情和友情， 并且会照顾伤病员、埋葬逝者。 这些发现改变了我们对尼安德特 人的看法。 请关注尼安德特人的第二讲吧， 我们将共同探索我们对于这些人 类的观念是如何改变的。

paragraphs.en: people has changed over time. Thank you very much.
paragraphs.zh: 谢谢。

titleZh: 第八讲：为什么尼安德特人身上总是伤痕累累？

paragraphs.en: Hello everyone, and welcome back to part II of our lessons on the Neanderthals. Last time , I told you that our conceptions of these people have changed over time as we have learned more about them. So let’s start by this lesson about how our artistic portraits of these people have changed over the years. In the 1910s, they were depicted as ape-like. Similar to monkeys or gorillas. By the 1920s, they were generally depicted as people, but as very stupid people. By the 1960s, they were depicted as smarter, but they were often made very hairy, which made them seem more like animals and less like men
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到我们关于尼安 德特讲座的第二部分。上一讲我 提到我们的观念随着我们对他们 的了解而改变了，那么我们本讲 就我们对他们复原图的改变进行 探究。 在 20 世纪 10 年代，他们还被画 成人猿，类似猴子或猩猩。 到了 20 年代，他们通常会被画成 人类，但是看起来智力低下。 到了 60 年代，尼安德特人被描绘 得聪明了一些，但是他们往往很 多毛，看起来更像是动物而不是 人。

paragraphs.en: and women. We can see a major advance by the 1980s. Here they are shown communicating and thinking. By the early 2000s they were seen as human, but like really tough primitive people. And by 2008, we had come to see Neanderthals as what they are, people like us. There is a painting of the Neanderthals from the 1950s that I do really like. And its this one of them fighting a cave bear by the artist Zdenik Burian. And although I think most Neanderthals would have avoided getting THAT close to a cave bear, it shows something about their lifestyles.
paragraphs.zh: 我们可以看到 20 世纪 80 年代有 一个重大的进步。这幅画展现了 尼安德特人交流和思考的样子。 到了 21 世纪初，他们看起来就是 人类的模样了，然而是非常粗犷 的原始人。 而到了 2008 年，我们可以看到， 尼安德特人已经是和我们一样的 相貌了。 这是一幅我非常喜欢的画作，于 20 世纪 50 年代由画家兹迪克·布 里安所绘。画的是尼安德特人与 洞熊战斗的场景。 尽管我认为大部分尼安德特人会 避免与熊如此近距离接触，但是 这幅画展示了他们生活方式。

paragraphs.en: You see, Neanderthal skeletons frequently show evidence of very severe injuries. This Neanderthal skull has teeth marks from a lion, these bones were broken and then healed. Evidence of injuries are extremely common among Neandethal skeletons. In the ancient world there were several types of weapons that you can use to kill at a distance: spear throwers, bows and arrows, bolas and sling. But as far as we know, Neanderthals had none of these weapons. They never developed the technology. Instead the Neanderthals relied on 1
paragraphs.zh: 你可以看到，尼安德特人的骨骼 常常带有严重的伤痕。 这具尼安德特人的骨骼带有狮子 的齿痕，这些骨头一度破碎后又 愈合。 在尼安德特人的骨头上，伤痕屡 见不鲜。 在远古时期，能用于远程杀伤的 武器不多：标枪、弓箭、流星锤， 还有投石机。 但是据我们所知，尼安德特人并 没有这些武器。 他们没有发展出这些先进技术。 他们依靠的是一种原始的武器。

paragraphs.en: primary weapon. Heavy stabbing spears with stone points. These weapons required you to get very close to the animal you were trying to kill. Close enough to stab this animal to death. And the Neanderthals killed big powerful animals like mammoths. Or wooly Rhinos. They got right up next to these creatures so that they could stab and club them to death. And getting that close to a big powerful animal and killing it with a heavy spear is dangerous work. Is it any wonder that the Neanderthals got injured so
paragraphs.zh: 带有石制尖端的重型长矛。 这种武器需要非常靠近猎物才能 发挥作用。 近到足以把动物戳死。 而尼安德特人可以猎杀强壮的大 型动物，比如猛犸象和长毛犀。 他们要近身攻击这些动物，才能 将他们戳死或敲死。 要如此靠近一头大型猛兽、并且 用重型长矛去捕杀它，是十分危 险的事情。 这会不会就是尼安德特人频繁受 伤的原因呢？

paragraphs.en: frequently? The other thing to know about Neanderthals is they did not live in Africa. Their range was limited to Europe, the Middle East, and Central Asia. And Neanderthals were a creature adapted to the ice ages. A time when Wooly mammoths, wooly rhinos and saber toothed tigers roamed the earth. Here's another picture of the kind of place where Neanderthals lived. Northern Europe in those days was covered with glaciers in those days. And to give you a sense of things. Here is what a glacier looks like today. But the ice-age glaciers were
paragraphs.zh: 关于尼安德特人，我们还需要知 道一点：他们并非生活在非洲。 他们的生活范围仅限于欧洲、中 东，及中亚地区。 而且尼安德特人适应了冰河时 代。 那时猛犸象、长毛犀和剑齿虎还 在大地上漫步。 这是另一副展现尼安德特人生活 环境的图片。 那时欧洲北部被冰川覆盖着。 为了让大家有一个直观的印象， 这就是今天的冰川的样子。 但是冰河时代的冰川厚达几千

paragraphs.en: thousands of meters tall. Here are the modern skylines of Toronto, Chicago, Boston and Montreal and the glaciers that covered them in the ice age. Look at Toronto. Do you see the CN tower in that picture of the skyline. Now look at how big it is compared to the glacier that was there during the ice age! But then, 300,000 years ago, while the Neanderthals were living and thriving in ice-age Europe, a cousin of theirs was evolving along different lines in the south in Africa: us. Both Neanderthals and Homo sapiens evolved from Homo Heidelbergensis.
paragraphs.zh: 米。 这是多伦多、芝加哥、波士顿和 蒙特利尔这几座现代大都市的天 际线，以及冰河时代覆盖于这些 地方的冰川。 请看多伦多。看到图片中的加拿 大国家电视塔了吗？ 现在看看它与冰河时代的冰川的 高度对比！ 然而就在那时，30 万年前，尼安 德特人在冰河时代的欧洲繁衍生 息。而他们的一支表亲，则在南 方的非洲沿着不同的脉络进化 着：那就是我们人类。 尼安德特人和智人都是海德堡人 的后裔。

paragraphs.en: But the Neanderthals evolved in Europe while we evolved in Africa. You can see the big difference if you compare our skeletons. Neanderthals are short and stocky, we modern humans are tall and lean by comparison. Here is what Neanderthals and we Homo sapiens might look like if we stood next to each other today. That shorter muscular Neanderthal frame was better at conserving body heat in ice-age Europe, and also better built to fight up close with the powerful animals that the Neanderthals hunted. One of the discoverieswas made in the Omo valley in southwestern Ethiopia. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, a
paragraphs.zh: 但是尼安德特人在欧洲进化，而 我们现代人则在非洲进化。 你可以看到两个人种骨骼的区 别。 尼安德特人矮小壮实，相比之下 我们现代人则更加高大瘦削。 如果尼安德特人和智人站在一 起，就会是这样的情形。 尼安德特人矮小且肌肉发达的体 型更利于在冰河时代保存身体的 热量，也更利于在狩猎时与猛兽 搏斗。 智人化石最早发现于埃塞俄比亚 西南部的奥莫山谷。 20 世纪 60 年代末 70 年代初，这

paragraphs.en: skull belonging to a modern Homo sapiens was discovered here. The fossils were 195,000 years old. Which at the time were the oldest bones from our species that had ever been discovered. But then, another discovery in Jebel Irhoud Morocco changed our thinking. Here we discovered a Homo sapiens skull that was about 315,000 years old! These are the oldest Homo sapiens bones that have ever been found. These are people who had brains and bodies just like ours. So as far as we know, our species is about 300,000 years old. Please join me next class as we
paragraphs.zh: 里出土了一枚现代智人的头骨化 石。 这枚化石有 19 万 5 千年的历史。 这在当时是已经出土的人类化石 中最古老的。 但是后来，另一个在摩洛哥的杰 贝尔·艾胡德的发现改变了我们 的想法。 在这里我们发现了有 31 万 5 千年 历史的智人头骨！ 这些就是目前已被发现的最古老 的智人骨骼化石。 这些人的头颅和身体与我们相 似。 因此据我们目前所知，我们人类 有大约 30 万年的历史。 欢迎继续加入我的下一堂课，我

paragraphs.en: discuss our own species, Homo sapiens and how we left Africa and spread across the Earth.
paragraphs.zh: 们将讨论我们人类这个物种—— 智人，以及我们是如何走出非洲， 遍布全球的。

titleZh: 第九讲：这次火山大爆发后，整个地球上只幸存了 10000 智人

paragraphs.en: Hello! Welcome back to our lecture series on Human History. Today we will be talking about our own species: Homo Sapiens! As I mentioned last time. For a long time, the oldest Homo sapiens bones ever discovered came from the Omo valley in southern Ethiopia. These fossils are about 200,000 years old. But more recently we have discovered bones from Jebel Irhoud that are about 300,000 years old. So this find had pushed back the history of our species by another 100,000 years.
paragraphs.zh: 10000 智人 大家好！欢迎回到我们的人类历史 系列讲座。 今天我们要讨论我们人类这个物 种：智人！ 上一讲中我提到过。 很长时间以来，最早的智人骨骼化 石发现于埃塞俄比亚南部的奥莫 山谷。 这些化石有大约 20 万年的历史。 但是最近我们在杰贝尔伊尔胡德 发现了约有 30 万年历史的骨骼化 石。 所以这次发现将我们人类的历史 又向前推移了 10 万年。

paragraphs.en: Another famous set of early fossils was also found in Ethiopia near a village called Herto. These are a little bit younger, from 160,000 years ago, but they are still enormously old bones for our species. Here is a picture of the skull on the right and an artist's depiction of the man it belonged to on the left. Now these early bones are interesting because they show that there were people with bodies like us and brains like us as far back as 200,000 years ago. BUT, these ancient humans did not seem to behave completely like us. The tools that they use do not seem to be noticeably different from those used by Homo
paragraphs.zh: 还有一处著名的早期化石遗迹也 发现于埃塞俄比亚，一个名叫埃尔 托的村落附近。 这些化石年代稍近，有 16 万年历 史，但是它们对人类而言仍然是非 常古老的骨骼化石。 这幅图片右侧是一枚头骨，左侧则 是艺术家绘制的头骨主人的样貌。 这些早期的骨骼化石能带给我们 很多有趣的发现，因为它们显示： 早在 20 万年前，就已经出现了躯 体和大脑与都我们相似的人类了。 但是，似乎这些古老的人类在行为 上与我们并不完全相似。 他们使用的工具看起来和海德堡 人的工具并没有明显差异。

paragraphs.en: Heidelberensis. But then, about 70,000 years ago, things began to change. To tell this story I need to jump across the world to the Island of Sumatra in Indonesia. On this island there is a big lake called Lake Toba. Here is picture of what that lake looks like today. However there is something special about this lake.It is actually not a normal lake, but the crater of a volcano that erupted 70,000 years ago. This was a super volcano, and this eruption is called the Toba eruption and it was huge. To understand just how big, let's
paragraphs.zh: 但是后来，在大约 7 万年前，情况 发生了变化。 要讲清楚这件事，我们需要来到世 界的另一端，先说说印度尼西亚的 苏门答腊岛。 这座岛上有一个名为托巴的大湖。 这张图片展示了这片湖泊今日的 风貌。 实际上，这不是寻常的湖泊，而是 一座 7 万年前喷发过的火山口。 这是一座超级火山，而这场火山爆 发被称为托巴火山大爆发，其规模 非常宏大。 要理解这场喷发的规模之大，我们

paragraphs.en: make a few comparisons. Here is a picture of several different volcanic eruptions. The mount Toba eruption is the one on the right. It threw 2800 cubic kilometers of dust into the atmosphere. By comparison, a famous recent volcanic eruption was Mount Saint Helens in 1980. Mt. Saint Helens threw just 1 cubic kilometer of dust into the air. So mount Toba was 3000 times more powerful and to get a sense of that, let’s think about Mount Saint Helens. This is a picture of Mount Saint Helens erupting. And here's another picture of the
paragraphs.zh: 可以做一些比较。 这张图片展示了几场不同的火山 爆发。 托巴火山大爆发在最右侧。 这场爆发向大气中抛洒了 2800 立 方千米的火山灰。 作为比较，最近一场比较著名的火 山爆发是 1980 年的圣海伦斯火山 爆发。 而圣海伦斯火山仅仅向空中抛洒 了 1 立方千米的火山灰。 所以托巴火山大爆发的威力大概 是圣海伦斯火山爆发的 3000 倍。 这是圣海伦斯火山喷发的情形。 这是另一张反映这场喷发的照片。

paragraphs.en: eruption. This is what a 1 cubic kilometer eruption looks like. The Mount Toba eruption was 3000 times bigger than that! The effects of the eruption were of course felt most strongly in South Asia and East Africa. But that's exactly where most humans were living 70,000 years ago! A huge dust cloud formed over this part of the world and ash fell from the sky thousands of kilometers away. By some estimates, the entire Indian subcontinent was covered by 30 centimeters of ash. Imagine that! 30 centimeters of ash covering ALL of India.
paragraphs.zh: 这就是 1 立方千米火山灰的样子。 而托巴火山大爆发的规模是这个 的 3000 倍！ 受这次喷发影响最强烈的地区无 疑是南亚和东非。 然而这恰恰是 7 万年前大多数人 类生活的地方！ 巨量的火山灰乌云笼罩着这片地 区，灰尘飘洒了数千公里。 据估算，整个印度次大陆都被 30 厘米厚的灰烬覆盖了。 想像一下那场面！整个印度都被 30 厘米厚的灰烬覆盖着！

paragraphs.en: But that wasn't even the worst part. The smaller particles didn't fall out of the sky; Instead they got carried high into the atmosphere and stayed there for several years. Up in the stratosphere they blocked sunlight and the Earth cooled down, causing a winter that lasted for several years. Imagine that, a winter that lasts for years. The planet was plunged into a mini ice age. Plants and animals began dying. And with them the humans who relied on them for food. Human beings across the planet began starving to death. In biology there is a term for an
paragraphs.zh: 但这还不是最糟糕的。 细小的微粒没有从空中飘落，而是 随气流被带到了大气层，并在那停 留了数年。 这些平流层中的小颗粒遮蔽了阳 光，导致地球温度下降，造成了一 场持续数年的寒冬。 想象一下，长达数年的冬季。 全球陡然进入冰河时代。 动植物开始死亡。 以这些动植物为食的人类也随之 消亡。 全球的人类开始饿死。 在生物学中有一个术语用以形容

paragraphs.en: event like this, it is called a bottle-neck. Because on a bottle the neck is where the bottle goes from very thick to very thin. In biology a bottle-neck event is when the population of a species goes from large to small very quickly. If you draw this change on a graph, it looks like a bottle-neck. After a bottle-neck event there are two possibilities, the last survivors die-off and the species goes extinct, or the population recovers. We humans went through a population bottle-neck after this eruption. By some estimates, our population dropped until there were only 10,000 survivors on the whole
paragraphs.zh: 这类事件，那就是“瓶颈”。 因为瓶颈是一只瓶子由粗到细的 部分。 在生物学中，瓶颈效应会发生于物 种数量锐减时。如果你在一张图表 上将这种变化画下来，那呈现出来 的样子就像是一个瓶颈。 瓶颈效应会造成两种可能的结果： 要么物种灭绝，要么种群恢复。 我们人类在这次火山爆发之后就 经历了这样一场人口瓶颈效应。 据估算，当时全球人类种群数量锐 减到只剩 1 万人。

paragraphs.en: Earth. Think about that! Only 10,000 human beings living on the whole planet. We were endangered species. Never again have we come so close to extinction as we did on that fate-full day, 70,000 years ago. This is why we human being are so closely related to each other. Because every single one of us is a descendant of those 10,000 survivors. You, me, everyone on Earth. We are only here today because somehow our ancestors found a way to survive a catastrophe of unimaginable proportions. And then, soon after the volcano,
paragraphs.zh: 想想那是什么情形！ 整个星球只剩下 1 万人了。 我们一度成了濒危物种。 自 7 万年前那个决定命运的时期 过后，我们再没有如此濒临灭绝 过。 这就是为什么我们人类互相之间 如此息息相关。 因为我们每个人都是那 1 万个幸 存者中某一位的后代。 你、我，还有地球上的每个人。 我们今天得以存在，正是因为我们 的祖先设法挺过了这场浩劫。 而后来，在火山大爆发不久之后，

paragraphs.en: something remarkable began to happen. And our oldest evidence of this comes from a cave on the South African coast called Blombos Cave. In this cave, paleontologists have found this rock. A piece of red-ochre with lines scratched into it. Now, I know this doesn't look like much at first, but here's why this little rock is important. Why did some ancient human carve those little lines into that rock? They serve no purpose. There is only one reason, he or she did it as a decoration, or as a symbol of something else. This little, unassuming rock, is the
paragraphs.zh: 发生了一件大事。 关于这件大事，最早的证据来自于 非洲南部海岸的一个名为布隆伯 斯的洞穴。 在这个洞穴中，古生物学家发现了 这块岩石。 一块带有刻痕的代赭石。 现在，我知道这块小石头看着不起 眼，但我要告诉你它为何如此重 要。 为什么远古的人要在这块石头上 刻这些线条呢？ 它们没什么实际作用。 只有一种可能，有人将这个作为装 饰，或者其他事物的象征。 这块不起眼的小石头，是艺术的最

paragraphs.en: very first example of art. This little rock represents the oldest thing we have of artistic or symbolic thinking. This little rock represents the birth of the imagination. Before Toba, we never find any sort of artistic or symbolic or decorative artifact. But after the eruption, we start finding them all the time. So how did those 10,000 people survive? They probably were not bigger or stronger or faster than the people who died. Those 10,000 survivors were the human beings who had the greatest capacity for imagination.
paragraphs.zh: 早实例。 这块小石头代表着人类有了艺术 或象征性思维。 这块石头还代表着想象力的诞生。 在托巴火山大爆发之前，我们从未 发现过任何形式的艺术品、象征 物，或装饰品。 但是在火山爆发后，我们开始持续 发现这类物品。 那么，这一万人是怎么幸存下来的 呢？ 他们或许并不比那些死去的人更 壮硕更敏捷。 这一万个幸存者是那些最具想象 力的人。

paragraphs.en: These were the people who could look at a problem and think up brand new solutions. They could figure out brand new ways to survive. They had the ability to imagine a different world. And they passed this ability onto their children, and we, those humans alive today, are their descendants. And this ability to imagine, to think creatively was what let those 10,000 people survive the eruption. And then once the last of the ash fell from the sky and the long winter ended, these survivors were ready to re-populate the Earth. Except this time they had a new capacity for imagination and
paragraphs.zh: 他们是可以审视问题并想出全新 解决方案的人。 他们能想出新的生存之道。 他们能想象出一个崭新的世界。 并且他们可以将这种能力传递给 他们的子孙，而我们——生活在今 天的人类，就是他们的后代。 而正是这种想象力让这一万人得 以从那场火山爆发中幸存下来。 后来，当最后一粒尘埃从空中飘 落，长冬结束了。这些幸存者已经 准备好在地球上繁衍生息。 这一次他们不但有了想象力和创 造性思维。

paragraphs.en: creative thought. And they began producing brand new tools and works of art. After Toba, there is an explosion in the creativity and diversity of the artifacts we discover. For example, in that same cave, we also find shells with little holes drilled in them. What was the purpose in doing this, well the shells can then be put on a string to make a necklace! These shells are the oldest jewelry in the world! In Germany, scientists discovered this figuring carved from a mammoth tusk. They call it the 'Venus of Hohle Fels'.
paragraphs.zh: 他们还开始制作全新的工具和艺 术品。 在托巴火山大爆发之后，我们还发 现，在手工制品的创造力和多样性 上，也发生了一场大爆发。 比如，在同一个山洞里，我们还发 现了打着小孔的贝壳。 这样做的目的在于，这些贝壳可以 穿起来做成项链！ 这些贝壳就是世界上最早的首饰！ 在德国，科学家还发现了这尊用猛 犸象牙雕成的雕像。 他们把它命名为“维伦多尔夫的维 纳斯”。

paragraphs.en: It is an abstract, exaggerated representation of a woman's body. And THIS is the very first work of sculpture. Its the oldest depiction of a human being that we have ever found. And this little figurine may also have had religious significance. Perhaps it was believed to confer fertility on women. If so, it also shows us the very beginning of religion and spiritual thought. We've also discovered THIS carving of a lion-man from roughly the same time period, 40,000 years ago. A creature that is half-lion and half-man of course cannot exist in
paragraphs.zh: 那是一个抽象、夸张的女性人体。 而这就是世界上第一件雕塑作品。 它还是目前发现的最古老的刻画 人类的作品。 这尊小雕像或许还有着宗教意义。 也许人们相信它能赋予女性生育 能力。 如果是这样，那么它还向我们展示 了宗教和精神思想的起源。 我们还发现了这件大约来自同一 历史时期——4万年前的狮头人身 像。 自然界中不可能存在半狮半人的 生物。

paragraphs.en: nature. Only a person with imagination can create something like this. And thousands of miles away, in Indonesia, we have also found the very first cave paintings. These are the impressions of human hands, but made by people who lived 1000 generations ago! The history of painting begins there in that cave. And we have also found this. A hollow bone with holes carved in it. It is a flute. And it is the world's oldest musical instrument. The history of music begins with that little piece of bone.
paragraphs.zh: 只有具有想象力的人才能创造这 样的东西。 在数千里之外的印度尼西亚，我们 还发现了第一件洞穴岩画。 这些是人类的手印，然而是由1000 代以前的人制作的！ 绘画的历史就始于这个洞穴。 而且我们还发现了这样一件物品。 一根钻了洞的空心骨头。 这是一支笛子。 而这就是世界上最古老的乐器。 音乐的历史就开始于这一小截骨 头。

paragraphs.en: We see beautiful cave paintings in France at the Chauvet or Lascaux caves. That painting, is 35,000 years old. Lions no longer live in France, but they did 35,000 years ago. And you know what, not only is that an old painting, I think its a good painting. I think they really look like lions! The artist who painted these had talent. Here is another one of those fertility goddess statues: probably the most famous of these ancient carvings. It all started with a few decorative lines carved into a piece of ochre, but then we started making jewelry, we began to paint, we started
paragraphs.zh: 我们在法国的肖维和拉斯科的洞 穴中还见到了漂亮的岩画。 这幅画有 35,000 年的历史。 如今法国已经没有野生狮子了，但 在 35,000 年前是有的。 你知道吗，这不仅是一幅很古老的 画，而且画得还很不错。 我觉得它们真的很像狮子！ 作者颇有绘画天赋。 这是另一尊生育女神雕像：沃尔道 夫的维纳斯。这或许是远古雕塑中 最著名的一件了。 这一切都始于一块代赭石上刻着 的几根线条，但之后我们开始制作 首饰、开始绘画、开始雕塑，并且 开始创作音乐。

paragraphs.en: carving and making sculpture, and we began making music. Our species did something that no humans had ever done in the history of our evolution: we made art! THAT is what made us different. The main skill modern Homo Sapiens seem to possess separates us from the other humans is the ability to think symbolically and imaginatively.We can imagine things that have necer existed.And then we can make them real.We see this very clearly in the beginning of art.But we also see this very clearly in our tools. But we will save that part of story for our next lesson, please join me next time for Homo sapiens part II, as we discuss the new types of tools our ancestors developed, we will
paragraphs.zh: 我们这个物种完成了我们进化史 上任何其他人种都没有做到的事 情：我们创造了艺术！ 这令我们与众不同。 符号思维和想象力是智人和其他 人类最关键的区别。我们可以想象 那些从未存在过得失误并把它们 创造出来。 下节课，也就是智人第二部分，我 们将继续共同讨论我们的祖先是 如何改良工具，以及他们是如何利 用这些工具，超越前人，见识到更 广阔的世界的。

paragraphs.en: discuss how these tools let them spread farther into the world than any human had ever gone before.

10：Homo Lecture Hello and welcome back to our lesson on human origins, last time in part I of the lesson on Homo sapiens we looked at how the Toba super volcano might have forced our spices to develop an imagination in order to survive. We looked at some of the art work that our new imaginative ancestors began to developed, but we also see the new ability to imagine and create very clearly in the types of tools Homo sapiens began to make. So to tody,we are going to look at those tools and the new world that they made possible. For example, we see these, stone blades. They are thin and sharp and allow
paragraphs.zh: 欢迎回到人类起源课程，在第一部 分我们讲述了托巴火山的爆发后， 人类为了生存而产生了想象力。 我们可以看到，充满想象力的智人 祖先创造了艺术品；从他们发明的 新工具中，我们还可以看到，他们 发展出了全新的想象力和创造力。 所以今天我们将焦点放在智人制 造的工具以及他们由此创造的世 界上。 比如，我们发现了这些石制刀刃。 他们很薄、很尖锐，可以用于精准

paragraphs.en: the user to cut much more precisely, like a knife. We also see these things, tiny stone tools called 'Microliths.' These may have been used in hunting or fishing, but whatever their usage, we are looking at a precision tool. And at the same time, we see the next big advance in the bigger stone tools. Instead of chipping the axes, people began to polish them. You take the stone, chip it into its general shape, but then you rub it against another wet rock in order to finish it off. This polishing technique resulted in axes that were stronger and sharper than ever.
paragraphs.zh: 的切割，就像刀子一样。 我们还见到了这种小的，被称为 “细石器”的石制工具。 这些工具可能是用于狩猎或捕鱼 的，但是无论他们有什么作用，我 们看到的都是一种十分精细的工 具。 与此同时，较大的石制工具也呈现 出又一场巨大的进步。 人们不再碎石作斧，而是开始打磨 这些斧子了。 你拿起一块石头，将其凿出个大概 的形状，之后你还有要拿它在另一 块润湿的石头上打磨，这样才能完 工。 这种打磨工艺造出的斧子达到了 前所未有的锋利度。

paragraphs.en: Look at this polished axe head. Isn't it beautiful? To me this is not just a tool, but a work of art. Here are some more examples of these polished stone tools. And we also see another advance in our technology. Humans began to use materials other than stone to make their tools. It is at this point that we start seeing to first bone tools. And the great thing about bone is that you can carve it into much more precise shapes. Its a nice in-between material. Harder and longer lasting than wood, but softer than rock.
paragraphs.zh: 请看这只打磨过的斧头。 是不是很漂亮？ 对我来说这不仅是一件工具，更是 一件艺术品。 这里还有其他这种打磨过的工具。 我们的技术也经历了又一次飞跃。 人类开始用岩石以外的材料制作 工具了。 正是从这一时期开始，我们发现了 最早的骨制工具。 骨头的最大优点在于你可以将它 雕刻成更加精细的形状。 这是一种良好的中性材料。 骨头比木头更加坚硬耐久，但是比 石头软。

paragraphs.en: You can use bone to make very precise tiny object like hooks and needles. Now it might look like a small thing, but don't underestimate how important the invention of the needle was. Needles allow us to sew clothes. And sewing allows us to make very warm clothes, which allows people to start living in the coldest parts of the world.
