It was the needle that allowed our species to live beyond the tropics! And bone tools allow us to make hooks and harpoons. The tools we need to fish. We think this is the period in history when humans first began fishing.
paragraphs.zh: 你可以用骨头制作非常细小的东 西，比如钩子和针。 这些东西看起来小，但是可不要低 估了针的重要意义。 针让我们可以缝制衣服。 而缝纫使我们能制作非常保暖的 衣物，让我们得以在世界上最寒冷 的区域开始生存。 正是针让我们人类能够在赤道以 外的地区生存。 并且骨制工具还让我们有了钩子 和鱼叉。 这些都是捕鱼所需的工具。 我们认为人类就是从这一时期开 始捕鱼的。

paragraphs.en: Think about what a difference fishing makes. A whole new food source became available to our ancestors. We also see the very first arrow-heads about 50,000 years ago. This meant that our ancestors had invented the bow and arrow. Think of how much easier and safer hunting becomes once you have a weapon like THAT. Certainly our ancestors had invented the bow and arrow by 20,000 years ago, because they drew it in their cave painting. We've also discovered another long-range weapon called the spear-thrower.
paragraphs.zh: 想想捕鱼能带来哪些改变吧。 我们的祖先有了一种全新的食物 来源。 我们还发现了5万年前的第一枚箭 镞。 这意味着我们的祖先已经发明出 了弓箭。 想象一下如果你有这样的工具，打 猎将变得多么轻松安全吧。 2 万年前，我们的祖先肯定已经发 明出了弓箭，因为他们将其画在了 岩画中。 我们还发现了另一种远程武器，叫 做长矛投掷器。

paragraphs.en: The carving on the left is a decorative depiction of a bull, but the important part is the little hook on the bottom. That hook is same as the hook you see in the picture on the right. This hook allows you to notch a spear onto the thrower. And then you hurl it like the man in this illustration. By using a spear-thrower you can increase your leverage and throw your spear much farther than you could with just your arm. This is ANOTHER ranged weapon that makes hunting big animals easier and safer. We've also found skeletons that were buried 23,000 years ago.
paragraphs.zh: 图片左侧的雕塑是一头装饰性的 公牛，但关键的部分是底部的小钩 子。 这枚钩子和你在图片右侧看到的 一样。 这枚钩子可以让你在上面固定一 只长矛。 然后你可以像这张图片上的人一 样将长矛甩出去。 通过这个长矛投掷器，你增加了力 臂，可以比徒手投得更远。 这是另一种使捕猎大型动物更加 轻松安全的远程武器。 我们还发现了2万3千年前被埋葬 的骸骨。

paragraphs.en: But in addition to being deliberately buried, they were ALSO covered with red ochre. This red-ochre is strong evidence of some sort of religious ceremony that accompanied the burial. This is some of the earliest evidence of ritual and religion. Other important effects of our new capacity for imagination and symbolic thought. So let's take a look of all of these changes. After the Toba eruption, a big change occurs in the types of artifacts we discover. After Toba we start seeing sculpture, painting, music, bone tools, advanced stone tools, fishing, and ritual burial.
paragraphs.zh: 他们不但是被有意埋葬的，还被覆 盖以代赭石。 这枚代赭石有力证明了葬礼会伴 随某种宗教仪式进行。 这是能证明仪式和宗教出现的最 早期证据。 并且也是我们新获取的想象力和 象征性思维的又一个重要影响。 那么让我们来看一看所有这些变 化吧。 在托巴火山大爆发之后，我们发现 的手工艺品有了巨大的变化。 在托巴火山大爆发之后我们开始 见到雕塑、绘画、音乐、骨制工具、 先进的石制工具、捕鱼，还有葬礼。

paragraphs.en: What do all of these things have in common? They all require creativity. That's what defines the last 50,000 years of human history. This period is defined by the emergence of the imagination. And with these new tools, Homo sapiens was able to spread farther than any Hominid had ever done before. About 200,000 years ago, our species began in Eastern and Northern Africa. By 100,000 years ago, we had spread throughout Africa and entered the middle east. At this point we came into contact with the Neaderthals.
paragraphs.zh: 这些事物有什么共同点呢？ 他们都需要创造力。 这定义了过去 5 万年的人类历史。 这段时期的特征就是想象力的产 生。 有了这些新工具，智人就能够比以 前的任何原始人走得更远。 在大约 20 万年前，我们这个物种 出现于非洲东部及南部。 到了 10 万年前，人类已经遍布非 洲，并且进入了中东地区。 就在这一时期我们与尼安德特人 产生了关联。

paragraphs.en: There is evidence that when we met our cousins we reacted in many different ways. Sometimes the encounters were violent and we killed each other. But at other times the meetings were loving, and we had babies together. Remember, there were not many human beings alive on Earth in that time. Meeting any type of new person would have been a rare and special event. For a long time, scientists argued over whether humans had mated with Neanderthals. But then in 2004, the Max Plank institute was able to get a sample of Neanderthal DNA, and run a
paragraphs.zh: 有证据表明，当我们与这些亲戚见 面时，我们在很多方面都有不同的 反应。 有时相会的场面非常暴力，我们会 互相杀戮。 但有时又会充满爱意，会共同养育 后代。 请记住 ，那时地球上的人类数量 可不多。 遇到任何一个新人种都是极其稀 罕而特别的事。 长久以来，科学家都在争论人类是 否曾和尼安德特人有姻亲关系。 但是在 2004 年，马克斯·普朗克 研究所获取了一份尼安德特人的 DNA 样本，并与世界各地人类的基

paragraphs.en: genetic comparison to humans from around the world. They discovered that people who are pure-blooded African do not have Neanderthal DNA, because the Neanderthals never lived in Africa. BUT every other group of humans has Neanderthal DNA. Asians, Europeans, Native Americans, they all have between 1-5% Neanderthal DNA. And Europeans have the most Neanderthal DNA because the Neanderthals lived mostly in Europe. Eventually the Neanderthals died out, but not before interbreeding with us. So both groups of humans are our ancestors.
paragraphs.zh: 因进行了比对。 他们发现纯种非洲人并不携带尼 安德特人的 DNA，因为尼安德特人 从未在非洲生活过。 但是其他地区的所有人都带有尼 安德特人的 DNA。 亚洲人、欧洲人、美洲土著人都携 带着 1-5%的尼安德特基因。 而欧洲人带有的尼安德特基因最 多，因为尼安德特人主要就生活在 欧洲。 虽然尼安德特人最终灭绝了，但他 们已经与我们血脉相连。 所以这两个人种都是我们的祖先。

paragraphs.en: The majority of our ancestors come from the African Homo Sapiens. But about 5% of our ancestry comes from the European Neanderthals. When you read about Neanderthals, remember, they were your ancestors too! So about 100,000 years ago, we had spread throughout Africa and we beginning to move into the Middle East. By 70,000 years ago we had spread through the Middle East, Central Asia, India and Southeast Asia. Then the Toba eruption happened and our species came close to extinction. However, ten-thousand people survived the catastrophe and these survivors were those who had the
paragraphs.zh: 我们的祖先大多数都是非洲的智 人。 但是有 5%的祖先是来自欧洲的尼 安德特人。 当你读到关于尼安德特人的介绍 时，请记住，他们也是你的祖先。 所以在大约 10 万年前，我们已经 遍布非洲，并开始向中东迁徙。 到了7万年前，我们已经遍布中东、 中亚、印度，和南亚地区。 后来发生了托巴火山大爆发，我们 这个物种一度濒临灭绝。 无论如何，一万人幸免于难。而这 些幸存者就是最具创造力和想象 力的那群人。

paragraphs.en: greatest creativity and strongest imaginations. After a few thousand years our numbers bounced back, but we now had a much greater capacity for imaginative thought, and you can see this in the art and tools our ancestors left behind. By 50,000 years ago, we had spread through Oceana and Australia. 40,000 years ago, our ancestors pushed into Europe and Northeastern Asia. It is around this time that the Neanderthals disappear from the fossil record. 30,000 years ago, we learned how to survive in very cold climates and our ancestors pushed north into Central Asia, Russia and Northern Europe, coming right up to the edge
paragraphs.zh: 几千年后我们的人口数量得到恢 复，但是此时我们已经拥有了更强 的想象力。你可以从我们祖先遗留 的艺术品和工具中窥见一斑。 到了 5 万年前，我们已经遍布大洋 洲和澳洲。 4 万年前，我们的祖先推进到了欧 洲和东南亚。 正是在这一时期，尼安德特人从化 石记录中消失了。 3 万年前，我们学会了在极寒地带 生存的本领，并且我们的祖先迈步 北上，进入了中亚、俄罗斯以及北 欧，直抵冰川边缘。

paragraphs.en: of the glaciers. About 15,000 years ago, an intrepid band of humans did what no hominid had ever done before. They entered the Americas by crossing over from Siberia. And by 12,000 years ago, they had pushed all the way into South America. 10,000 years ago, the last ice-age ended, and the giant glaciers melted. Humans quickly occupied the spaces left behind by the glaciers. So 10,000 years ago, our conquest of the Earth was complete. Except for several remote islands, our ancestors were now living in every corner of the planet.
paragraphs.zh: 在大约 1 万 5 千年前，一群勇敢的 人类做了所有原始人都未曾做过 的事。 他们跨越西伯利亚进入了美洲。 到了 12,000 年前，他们已经进入 了南美洲。 一万年前，最后一个冰河时代终 结，巨型冰川消融了。 人类很快占领了此前被冰川覆盖 的地区。 因此，在一万年前，我们对地球的 征服宣告完成。 除了几个偏远的岛屿，我们的祖先 此时已经生活在全球的各个角落。

paragraphs.en: By 10,000 years ago, the Neanderthals and all other human species had disappeared. Modern Homo sapiens were now living everywhere on Earth. And now the ice-ages were over, and the entire planet had been occupied. The stage was now set for the next step in our journey: the invention of farming. So please join me next time as we discuss how our ancestors invented an entirely new way of living.
paragraphs.zh: 到了一万年前，尼安德特人和所有 其他人种都已消失了。 现代智人生活在了世界各地。 而且此时冰河时代已经结束，整个 地球都已被占领。 这为我们历史进程的下一个阶段 奠定了基础：农耕的发明。 那么欢迎继续收听我的下一讲。我 们将讨论我们的祖先是如何创造 一种全新的生活方式的。

titleZh: 第十一讲：把祖先埋在自家客厅底下的纳图夫人

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our next lecture on Human origins: the Agricultural Revolution. Our story starts in the nation of Israel, in the north near a mountain called Mt. Carmel. Here is a picture of Mt. Carmel today. Now if you explore Mt. Carmel, you will discover that there are caves up in the mountainside. In 1929, Dorothy Garrod, one of the first female paleontologists decided to explore those caves looking for ancient human skeletons. She was hoping to find skeletons that was about 50,000 years old , but instead, she found a skeleton
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史·人类 起源系列课程：农业革命。 我们的故事从以色列北部的迦密 山附近开始。 这是今天迦密山的照片。 现在，如果你探索迦密山，你会发 现山腰上有洞穴。 1929 年，第一批女性古生物学家 之一的多萝西·加罗德决定探索那 些洞穴，寻找古人类的骨骼。 她本打算寻找约 50,000 年前的化 石，但是她发现了一具 14,000 年 前的骨骼化石。

paragraphs.en: that was about 14,000 years old. Here is the skeleton she found: this person was buried around 12,000 BC. And if you look closely, you'll noticed that its head has a wrapping of a bind of woven shells around it. Here's another photo of Garrod's skeleton and a close picture of the woven headband. The shells came from the Denatlium mollusk, which forms long hollow shell. Somebody had collected these shells and then painstakingly woven them together as you can see in this modern re-creation. This jewelry was so distinctive that Dorothy Garrod believed she had discovered a new culture of people. She called them 'the Natufians'
paragraphs.zh: 这就是她发现的骨骼：这个人大约 被埋葬于公元前 12,000 年。 如果你近距离观察，就会发现它的 头上包裹着一圈编织过的贝壳。 这是加罗德发现的骨架的另一张 照片，还有一张编织头巾的近照。 这些贝壳来自 the Denatlium mollusk，在它们的成长过程中形 成了空的贝壳。有人收集了这些贝 壳，并精心编织在一起，就像你在 现代再创造中所看到的那样。 这种珠宝是如此的与众不同，以至 于多萝西·加罗德相信她发现了一 种新的人类文化。 她将他们称为“纳图夫人”。

paragraphs.en: They were named after the Natuf valley, which is a little south of Mt. Carmel, and is also another site where more of these skeletons were discovered. But most importantly, buried next to the man with the headband was this object. It was a piece of antelope bone, with three long thin blades of flint set inside of the bone. Chemical analysis of this tool revealed that it had been used to cut grass. It was a sickle! Dorothy Garrod was looking at the oldest sickle in the world, a tool especially designed for cutting grass. If the Natufians developed special tools for cutting grass, they must have been harvesting lots and lots of local grasses. Probably they were
paragraphs.zh: 它们的名字取自纳塔图夫山谷，它 位于迦密山的南部，也是另一个发 现更多这些骨骼的地方。 但最重要的是，埋在带头巾的人旁 边的，就是这件物品。这是一块羚 羊骨，骨头里有三根细长的燧石 刀。对这一工具的化学分析显示， 它曾经被用来割草。 这是一把镰刀！多萝西·加罗德看 到的是世界上最古老的镰刀，这是 一种专门用来割草的工具。 如果纳图夫人开发了专门用来割 草的工具，他们一定是收获了大量 的草地。 可能他们正在收集食物 的种子。毕竟，你不会花时间为你

paragraphs.en: collecting the seeds for food. After all, you don't take the time to build a special tool for something you only do rarely. So this raises a question, WHY were the Natufians harvesting wild grass seeds？ Well, the answer to that question all has to do with the shifting climate.15,000 thousands years ago, in the year 13,000 BC, the world was still deep in the last ice age. We know this because of those ice-core records I told you about in the 3rd lecture. Here is a graph of the ice-core data for the last 15,000 years. The left side shows the the average temperature of Greenland. Today it's about negative 32˚ Celsius. In the ice ages, it was about negative
paragraphs.zh: 不常做的事情制造一种特殊的工 具。 这就引出了一个问题，为什么纳图 夫人会收割野生草籽？ 这个问题的答案与不断变化的气 候有关。15,000 年前，即公元前 13,000 年，地球仍处于冰河时代。 我们能够知道这一点，归功于我在 第三课讲过的那些冰芯记录。 这是过去 15,000 年来的冰芯数 据。左边显示的是格陵兰岛的平均 温度。现在的温度约为零下 32 摄 氏度。冰河时代的气温约为零下 45 摄氏度。

paragraphs.en: 45˚ Celsius. As you can can see from the chart, the planet was deep in an ice-age in the year 13,000 BC. But then, just 500 years later, it began to warm up dramatically, and the Earth entered a warm period. During this warm period, one of the best places for humans to lived was this stretch of land which includes modern Egypt, Israel, Palestine, Lebanon, Syria and Iraq.It was warm, and much wetter than it is today. There were plenty of animals to hunt and wild plants to gather. Historians call this region the 'Fertile Crescent'. Hunter gatherers who arrived in this region 14,500 years ago would have found it teeming with wildlife, but also covered with vast fields of
paragraphs.zh: 如你所见，公元前 13,000 年，地 球正深陷于冰河时期。 仅 500 年后，地球开始急剧升温， 进入了一个温暖的时期。 在这段温暖的时期，人类的最佳居 住地之一就是这片土地，包括如今 的埃及、以色列、巴勒斯坦、黎巴 嫩、叙利亚和伊拉克。天气很暖和， 比现在要潮湿多了。有大量的动物 可以狩猎，野生植物可供采集。历 史学家称这个地区为“新月沃土”。 14,500年前到达这一地区的狩猎- 采集者会发现这里到处都是野生 动物，同时也覆盖着大片的草地。

paragraphs.en: grass. Hunter-gatherers have to be smart and resourceful to survive, and they are usually very intelligent about how to find food in their environment.So the hunter gatherers experimented with these grasses to see if they were useful. They discovered that the seeds of some of these grasses were edible. After figuring out which seeds were edible and which were not, they quickly set to work gathering the edible ones. During the autumn they gathered as many of the edible grasses as they could and took the seeds back to their camp. Here is why the grass seeds were so valuable.1 adult can harvest 1000 kilograms of grass seeds in 3 weeks
paragraphs.zh: 狩猎-采集者必须足够足智多谋才 能生存，而且他们通常非常擅长在 各自的环境中找到食物。因此，狩 猎-采集者们尝了尝这些草，来判 断它们是否有用。 他们发现有些草的种子是可以食 用的。在弄清楚哪些种子是可食用 的，哪些不可食用之后，他们很快 就开始着手收集可食用的种子。 秋天，他们尽可能多地采集可食用 的草，并把种子带回营地。 这就是为什么草籽如此有价值。一 个成年人可以从 3 周的艰苦工作 中收获 1000 公斤的草籽。现代考

paragraphs.en: of hard work. Modern archaeologists have done experiments to prove this. 1000 kilograms of grass seeds contains 3.4 million calories of food energy. 3.4 million calories of food energy can feed 4 people for a year! And grass seeds are special for one more reasons. Unlike all the other food the Natufians could find, grass seeds do not spoil. If you keep them dry, they will last for 25 years or longer! In short, 3 weeks of hard work could feed your family for a year! Imagine you are living always just a few meals away from starvation. Suddenly you discover a new food that will feed your family for the
paragraphs.zh: 古学家已经通过实验来证明了这 一点。 1000 公斤的草籽含有 340 万卡路 里的能量。 340万卡路里的能量可以养活4 个 人一整年！ 草籽之所以非常特殊，还有一个原 因。与纳图人所能找到的其他它食 物不同，草籽不会变质。 如果你让它们保持干燥，它们可以 保存 25 年，甚至更久！ 简而言之，3 周的辛苦工作可以养 活你的家人一年！想象一下，你的 生活总被饥饿的威胁笼罩着。突 然，你发现了一种新的食物，它可 以为你的家人提供一整年的食物。 你不再需要担心饥饿。你会开始收

paragraphs.en: whole year. You no longer have to worry about starvation. Would you start collecting that new food? I certainly would. It makes complete sense that the Natufians began eating the grasses. But we modern people are not as familiar with grass seeds as our ancestors, so I have a hard time imagining what 1000 kilograms looks like. I imagine you also might have a hard time getting a picture in your head. So I went to the grocery store. They don't have wheat seeds at my local grocery store, but they do have rice, which is similar. This bag of rice that I'm holding is 10 kilograms.
paragraphs.zh: 集这些新食物吗？ 我一定会。纳图夫人开始吃草是完 全有道理的。 但是我们现代人并不像我们的祖 先那样熟悉草籽，所以我很难想象 1000 公斤是什么样子的。 我想，你也可能很难在脑海中找到 这样一幅画面。 因此我去了商店。我家附近的商店 里没有小麦种子，但他们有销售比 较相似的大米。 我拿着的这袋大米重 10 公斤。

paragraphs.en: So imagine 100 of these bags. THAT is the size of 1000 kilograms of wheat seeds. There are about 100 yellow bags in the picture on the right.So all of those bags together are equal to 1000 kilograms. Of course this changes things, once you have collected 1000 kilograms of seeds, it is impossible to keep moving around. You have to store your seeds and start living near your storage. And that is exactly what the Natufians did. They took their seeds back to their camp and stored them for the next year. And they built house out of wood,brush and stone.These were the first permanent settlements in the Middle East.
paragraphs.zh: 想象 100 个这样的袋子。那就是 1000 公斤草籽的样子。 我在超市大米区拍下的图片。这堆 大米一共 100 袋，每袋 10 公斤， 整个这一堆计重约 1000 公斤。 当然，一旦你收集了 1000 公斤的 种子，你就不可能继续四处走动 了。你必须储存你的种子，并且开 始住在你的储藏室附近。 纳图夫人正是这么做的。他们把种 子带回营地，并把它们储存起来， 以备来年使用。 他们用木头、茅草和石头，建立齐 了人类在中东求的第一个永久聚 居地。

paragraphs.en: These settlements were small,only a few houses with a few dozen people at first. But they were a big change in the history of our species. The Natufians had created the world's first villages. The map on the left shows you where they lived and extended their culture. Also you should remember, the population of the series was still very small, many scientists think there were only a thousand families in the entire Natufian region. The picture on the right,is the ruins of one of their ancient houses. The other thing I should mention is that Natufians did not only eat grain. They worked really hard harvesting and storing it for 1 month every year. But in the other
paragraphs.zh: 早期的聚居地非常小，只有几栋茅 屋，几十个居民。 但它们是我们物种历史上的一个 重大变化。纳图夫人创造了世界上 第一个村庄。 左边的地图向我们展示了他们是 如何生活和扩展他们的文化的。当 然，当时的人口还不很多，许多科 学家认为那时整个纳图夫地区只 有约 1000 个家庭。 右图显示的是纳图夫人当时住所 的遗存。 值得一提的是，纳图夫人也不是只 吃谷物。他们每年都非常努力地花 一个月时间收集并储存草籽。但在 接下来的 11 个月里，他们继续寻 找其他食物。毕竟，草籽能让你远

paragraphs.en: 11 months they continued looking for other food. After all, grass seeds will keep you from starving, but you need a mixed diet to stay healthy. The Natufians continued to go out and gather wild plants like almonds, dates, acorns, grapes and pistachios. They also continued to hunt big animals like gazelles, goats, boars and aurochs. The aurochs are the ancestors to the cow.Really they hunted anything they could catch. And they were skilled hunters too. We should think of the Natufians as settled hunter gatherers who ate grass seeds as a staple of their diet, but also continued looking for other foods . The Natufians also learned that they could crush the grass seeds in
paragraphs.zh: 离饥饿，但你还需要其它食物来保 持健康。 纳图夫人继续外出采摘诸如杏仁、 枣、橡子、葡萄和开心果等野生植 物。 他们还继续猎杀大型动物，如瞪 羚、山羊、野猪和野牛。真的，他 们猎杀能抓到的任何东西。他们也 是熟练的猎手。 我们应该把纳图夫人看作是定居 的狩猎-采集者，他们把草籽作为 他们日常饮食的主食，但同时也在 继续寻找其他食物。 纳图夫人还学会了用两块石头压 碎草籽，做成面粉。

paragraphs.en: between two stones, making a flour. They then mixed the flour with water and baked it on the hot rocks around the fire. The Natufians had just created the world's first bread. They shared this food with each other as they sat around the fire at meal-time. And because the Natufians had this store of food and the grain seeds and the grass seeds, the grain, they did not have to spend all their time hunting and gathering, so they have plenty of time to relax and make art. The Natufians loved to carve stone, and they turned their mortars from simple tools for grinding grain into
paragraphs.zh: 然后，他们把面粉和水混合在一 起，然后在火周围的热岩石上烘 烤。 纳图夫人创造了世界上第一个面 包。 当他们围坐在篝火旁的时候，他们 彼此分享着这些食物。 归功于纳图夫人的草籽储备技术， 他们不需要把所有的时间都花在 狩猎和采集上。因此他们有时间去 发展艺术。 纳图夫人喜欢雕刻，他们把研钵从 简单的工具变成了漂亮的物品。

paragraphs.en: beautiful object. They also carved beautiful jewelry like this necklace. And they carved little stone figurines. This one is called the Ain-Sakhri Lovers and it is the oldest artistic depiction in history of a couple embracing each other. We also know the Natufians had special burial practices. When a member of their family died, they often dug a hole in the middle of the floor in their house. Then they placed their family member in the hole. Then they placed a heavy river stone on their chest. I'm guessing this was to keep their soul anchored to the house. And then they filled in the hole. The
paragraphs.zh: 他们还雕刻了漂亮的珠宝，就像这 条项链。 他们还雕刻了小雕像。这个叫做 “艾因-萨赫里情人”，是历史上 最古老的描述一对夫妇拥抱的艺 术作品。 我们还知道纳图夫人有特殊的殡 葬习俗。当他们的一个家庭成员去 世时，他们经常在自家的地板上挖 一个洞，然后把去世的家人放在洞 里。 然后在胸口放上一块沉重的河石。 我猜这是为了把灵魂留在房子里。 然后他们把洞填满。纳图夫人把尸

paragraphs.en: Natufians buried their dead in the middle of their houses, directly beneath the fireplace. This might seem strange to us modern people at first. But think about it. It means that even though your family members have died, they are still close to you. Their souls still live in the house with you, watching over you and all of their descendants. This must have made their houses places of deeply spiritual significance. Your house is not just your home, but the resting place of your ancestors. We also found another interesting Natufian skeleton from this time period. The woman was buried curled up, but whoever buried her also placed
paragraphs.zh: 体埋在他们的房子中间，直接埋在 壁炉下面。 乍一看，这对我们现代人来说似乎 有些奇怪。但仔细想想，这意味着 即使你的家人已经去世，他们仍然 和你非常亲近。他们的灵魂仍然和 你一起住在房子里，看着你和他们 所有的后代。这一定使他们的房子 具有了深刻的精神意义。 你的房子不再只是你的家，也是你 祖先的安息之地。 我们还发现了另一具有趣的纳图 夫人骨架。 这名妇女被蜷缩着埋葬起来，但埋 葬她的人还把她的手放在小狗的

paragraphs.en: her hands around the body of a puppy dog. We have some evidence that dogs were domesticated earlier perhaps as long as 30,000 years ago. But the Natufian puppy-dog burial is our first proof. We know that the Natufians were sharing their world with man's best friend. But then, a disaster struck. So please join me for the next class when we talk about this disaster and we'll talk about how the Natufians changed and adapted to it, because the changes they made would in a sense make our world.Thank you.
paragraphs.zh: 尸体上。 我们有一些证据表明，狗可能早在 3 万年前就被驯养了。但是，纳图 夫人埋葬小狗尸体，是第一份证 据。 我们知道纳图夫人与人类最好的 朋友共享这个世界。 就在这时，灾难发生了。 请和我一起学习下节课，我们将讨 论纳图夫人是如何改变并适应这 场灾难的，从某种意义上来说，他 们做出的改变创造了我们的世界。 谢谢。

titleZh: 第十二讲：灾难又发生了，这次人类发明了农业

paragraphs.en: Hello, and welcome back to our lecture series on world history, last time I talked to you about how the ice age ended, the middle east became warm, grasses grew everywhere and hunter-gathers called Natufians arrived and learned how to eat the grasses and then because they had collected so many grasses, they settled down in one place where they buried their dead and lived for generation after generation. They also, we know domesticated dogs this time if dog had not already been domesticated. So, that's we learn last time. And then I told you a disaster destroyed these people, and that's what we are going to talk about in this lesson.
paragraphs.zh: 欢迎回到世界历史讲座！上节课我 们讲到冰河时代的结束，中东重新 变得温暖，纳图夫人的到来和他们 对于植物的食用与收集并且因此 定居。我们也提到了他们如何安顿 下来、埋葬死者，并且一代一代地 生活在这里。 我们也知道，他们这时驯化了狗 （如果这时狗还没有被驯化的话）， 我们之前也提到了威胁它们生存 的灾难，这节课我们将就这些内容 来进行讨论。

paragraphs.en: This whole Natufians way-of life was built around harvesting the wild grass seeds. This was possible because the ice age had ended and the world became warm and wet again, around the year 12,500 BC. But then after 1600 years of this kind of life, the climate shifted once more, and Earth plunged back into a mini-ice age, which scientists call: 'The Younger Dryas.' The glaciers in Europe returned. The planet cooled down and the ocean levels fell. In the middle east a terrible drought arrived that lasted for years. This would have caused the grass-lands to turn into deserts.
paragraphs.zh: 纳图夫人的生活方式是建立在收 集野生草籽的基础之上的。 这种方式之所以可行，是因为大约 在公元前 12,500 年，冰河时代已 经结束，世界又变得温暖潮湿。 但是，在经历了 1600 年的这样的 生活之后，气候再次发生了变化， 地球又重新回到了一个小冰河时 代，科学家们称之为“新仙女木 期”。 欧洲的冰川又重新出现了。 地球冷却下来，海平面也下降了。 在中东，出现了一场持续数年的严 重旱灾。 草地变成了沙漠。突然间，纳图夫 人的核心食物消失了。

paragraphs.en: Suddenly the food which had been at the center of the Natufian world, disappeared. There were no more giant fields of grass to go harvest each autumn. Just dry wasteland. The Natufians had no choice but to abandon their homes and look for food. Imagine what it must have felt like to be leaving the homes where your family had lived for 60 generations. Where all of your ancestors were buried. It must have been a sad and desperate time for the Natufian people. But the alternative was starvation, so they left. Some of these Natufians groups
paragraphs.zh: 每年秋天都不再有大片的草地可 供采摘，只剩干涸的荒地。 纳图夫人别无选择，只能放弃自己 的家园，寻找食物。 想象一下，离开你祖上 60 代生活 的家园，你会有怎样的感受。你所 有的祖先都埋葬在这里。 对于纳图夫人而言，这一定是个悲 伤而绝望的时刻。 但如果不离开，就将面临饥饿。所 以他们还是离开了。 一部分纳图夫人从山间搬到了湖

paragraphs.en: moved down out of the hills to the lakes. These lakes had shrunk a little during the drought, but they were still wet. However, the Natufians knew that wild grasses would not grow naturally in these places. But they had gotten used to eating grass seeds, it had been the staple of their diet for generations. And so they conducted a radical experiment. They dug up soil, and sacrificed some of their precious food stores to become seeds. They were deliberately planting the grass now. And it worked. The grass needed
paragraphs.zh: 边。 这些湖泊在干旱期缩小了一点，但 仍然有水。 然而，纳图人知道，在这些地方， 野草不会自然生长。 但是他们已经习惯了吃草籽，这是 他们世世代代的主食。 所以他们进行了一次激进的尝试。 他们挖出土壤，牺牲了一些珍贵的 食物储备，成为种子。 他们现在正在主动种草。 他们成功了。想要获取食物，他们

paragraphs.en: much more attention that before. They had to prepare the soil each year. They had to water the grass. They had to fight the weeds. BUT the grass gave them enough food to eat. They were not going to starve. They had become the world's first farmers! This is a change that has happened only a few times in all of human history. And the Natufians were the first to figure it out. The later Natufians are the first settled farmers in history. They grew 8 different crops. So these are the very oldest domesticated plants. They are: Emmer Wheat, Einkorn Wheat, Barley, Lentils, Peas, Chickpeas, Bitter Vetch and Flax.
paragraphs.zh: 需要花更多功夫。他们每年都要预 备土壤；他们得给草浇水；他们不 得不与杂草作斗争。但是草为他们 提供了充足的食物。他们不至于挨 饿。 他们成为了世界上第一代农民！ 这是一个在人类历史上为数不多 的重大变化。纳图夫人是第一批成 功的人。 后来的纳图夫人是第一批定居的 农民。 他们种植了 8 种不同的作物。这些 是最古老的种植植物。它们分别 是：二粒小麦，一粒小麦，大麦， 扁豆，豌豆，鹰嘴豆，苦荞麦和亚 麻。

paragraphs.en: We call these 8 plants the Neolithic Founder Crops, because agriculture was founded upon them. However, this new way of life was harder than their old way of life. Look at these early Natufians grind-stones on the left. The Natufians spent a lot of time carving their mortars into things of beauty. But the later Natufians just used a rock and created their mortars by using them. Living as a farmer was harder than living as a hunter-gatherer. They now had to spend time taking care of and protecting their crops. There was much less time for the Natufians to make art or decorate their tools.
paragraphs.zh: 我们把这 8 种植物称为新石器时 代的创始作物，因为农业是建立在 它们之上的。 然而，这种新的生活方式比他们以 前的生活方式更难。 看看左边这些早期的纳图夫人用 来磨粮食的石头。他们花了很多时 间把研钵雕刻得如此精美。 但后期的纳图夫人只是把石头敲 碎来制作研钵。 农民生活比以狩猎采集要困难得 多。 他们现在不得不花时间照顾和保 护他们的庄稼。 纳图夫人用来制作艺术品或装饰 工具的时间变少了。

paragraphs.en: And this is true all across the world. Whenever hunter-gatherers settle down become farmers, their quality of life gets worse, and become farmers, their quality of life gets worse. They eat a less nutritious diet. Much more grain and much less meat, fruit and vegetables. We can see this in their skeletons. Farmers are usually shorter than hunter gatherers and their bones are weaker, as you can see in their picture. But farming can feed A LOT more people. For example, according to Rouch estimates, 1 square kilometer of farmland can feed about 100 people using
paragraphs.zh: 这在世界各地都是如此。每当狩猎 采集者定居下来，成为农民时，他 们的生活质量就会变差。 他们的饮食营养不足，谷物更多， 肉类、水果和蔬菜较少。 我们可以在他们的骨骼中看到这 一点。农民通常比狩猎采集者矮， 他们的骨骼也较弱，正如你在他们 的照片中所看到的。 但是农业可以养活更多的人。 比如说，根据鲁什的统计， 用传统的方法，1 平方公里的农田 可以养活大约 100 人。

paragraphs.en: traditional methods. BUT 1 square kilometer of wilderness can only feed 1 hunter-gatherer. So once you switch to farming, your land can support 100 times as many people. Since farming can feed many more people! Farmers began having more children. Hunter gatherers families only have 2-3 children, because you cannot support that many babies when you are moving around all the time, then you have to carry them with you, and it takes children 18 years to learn enough skills as a hunter gatherer to bring in more food than they eat. But farmer families can put their
paragraphs.zh: 但是 1 平方公里的荒地只能养活 一个狩猎-采集者。 所以一旦你转向农业，你的土地可 以养活 100 倍的人口。 由于农业可以养活更多的人，农民 开始生育更多的孩子。 狩猎采集者的家庭只有 2-3 个孩 子，因为你不能养活这么多的孩 子，也不能带着他们到处漂泊，而 且他们要花 18 年的时间才能学会 足够的技能来获得食物。 但农民家庭的孩子只要会说话，会

paragraphs.en: children to work as soon as they can walk and talk. So farmers might have 10-12 children. After all, many hands makes light work. This creates a cycle. More farming, leads to more food, which leads to more people, which requires more farmland, which leads to more food, and more people, and more farming, and so on. After a few generations, the population has really grown, and at that point it becomes impossible to go back to hunting and gathering even if you want to because there were just no other way to get enough food to feed all these people. Once you start farming you are stock in that way of life. And of course, these small villages began to grow. More land was
paragraphs.zh: 走路，就可以工作。因此农民家庭 通常会有 10-12 个孩子。毕竟众人 拾柴火焰高。 这就创造了一个循环。 更多的农业，会带来更多的食物， 从而导致更多的人，这就需要更多 的耕地，从而导致更多的食物，等 等。 几代人之后，人口增长显著，就不 可能再回到仅靠打猎采集来维持 生活的状态了，没有其他的方式来 养活全部这些人了，一旦开始了农 作，就与这样的生活绑定了。 当然，这些小村庄也开始成长起 来。更多的土地变成了田地，建造

paragraphs.en: turned into fields, more houses were built. Pretty soon these Natufian villages have turned into the world’s first towns. And by a town I mean a place that has more than a thousand people living in it. And that brings us to the next stage of our story. The Neolithic Era in the Middle East. Please join me next time as we explore the effects of farming on this part of the world. Thank you everyone.
paragraphs.zh: 了更多的房屋。 很快，这些纳图夫村庄变成了世界 上最早的城镇，有超过千人居住的 城镇。 这就引出了我们故事的下一个阶 段：中东的新石器时代。 下一节课，请和我一起探讨农业对 这一地区的影响。 谢谢大家。

titleZh: 第十三讲：人类最早驯化的 8 种谷物和 8 种动物

paragraphs.en: Hello and welcome back to our lecture series on World History. Today we will be discussing a period in our past called the Neolithic. First let's do a little review. From last class, as you may remember, around 10,900 BC the Earth suddenly cooled off and entered another small ice-age called the 'Younger Dryas'. During this time, the glaciers expanded and the Earth became colder and drier. In the Middle East a terrible drought caused the large grasslands to dry up, and the Natufians lost their main source of food.
paragraphs.zh: 8 种谷物和 8 种动物 大家好，欢迎回到世界历史系列讲 座。 今天我们将讨论关于新石器时代 的历史。 首先让我们就上一讲内容做个小 回顾。大概公元前 10,900 年地球 气温骤降，进入了另一个叫做“新 仙女木期”的小冰川时期。 在这段时期内，冰川扩张，地球变 得更冷、更干燥。 在中东，一场可怕的旱灾导致草原 消失，纳图夫人失去了他们主要的 食物来源。

paragraphs.en: They had to leave their homes in order to survive. A few of the Natufians moved down into the lower areas where it was still wet. The grasses that they liked to eat did not grow here naturally, but the Natufians dug up the earth and planted their grass seeds. This was a radical change, but it worked and the grass grew. Now the Natufians had to work very hard to protect and tend their fields, but they found a way to survive, and by doing this, they became the world's first farmers. So farming began at this time around the year 10,000 BC, in the Jordan valleys. These numbers and locations are
paragraphs.zh: 为了生存他们不得不背井离乡。 一些纳图夫人迁到了更低的地方， 那里仍然湿润。 他们喜欢吃的植物在这里本来并 不自然生长，但是他们挖开泥土种 下种子。 这是一个重大的变化，但是奏效 了，植物长了出来。于是纳图夫人 必须要努力劳作保护与开垦土地 了，但是他们找到了一种生存的方 法。借由这种方法，他们成为了世 界上的第一批农耕者。 于是在约公元前 10000 年，农作在 约旦河谷开始了。 由于这些事件年代太过久远，这些

paragraphs.en: estimates of course, since it happened so long ago. These first farmers planted 8 species of plants known as the Neolithic founder crops. They are: Emmer wheat, Einkorn wheat, Barley, Lentils, Peas, Chickpeas, Bitter Vetch, and Flax. As they grew these crops, the first farmers selected the seeds from the plants that produced the biggest seeds and the most seeds and the best tasting seeds. Over hundreds of years they changed the DNA of the plants themselves as certain genes were selected and other genes were selected against. This process is known as domestication. For example here is a picture of wild wheat.
paragraphs.zh: 数字和位置当然是估算的。 这些初代农耕者被叫做新石器时 代的奠基作物分为八类：二粒小 麦，一粒小麦，大麦，扁豆，豌豆， 鹰嘴豆，苦荞麦和亚麻。 在他们种庄稼的时候，这些初代农 耕者学会了将作物果实中最饱满、 最丰实、最好吃的种子挑选出来。 几百年来，他们通过选择基因改变 了作物的 DNA，这个过程即是驯 化。 比如，这是一张野生小麦的图片。

paragraphs.en: And here is a picture of domesticated wheat. See how much bigger it is and how many more seeds it have. This is what happens if you are selecting wheat generation after generation, only choosing the seeds form the plants which have the most seeds and the best seeds. You are changing the DNA of the plant itself. Here is another picture of the same thing. The wild wheat is on the left, and the domesticated wheat is on the right. But as we mentioned last time, we did not just domesticate plants, we also domesticated animals. The very first domesticated animal is the dog. We have evidence that
paragraphs.zh: 这是驯化后的小麦。 它变大了这么多。 于是你在一代一代的作物中选择 种子最多、最饱满的，就这样改变 着植物本身的 DNA。 这是另外的一张图片,左边的是野 生小麦，右边的是驯化后的小麦。 但是就如我们上次提到的，我们并 不仅仅驯化植物，我们还驯化动 物。 最早的驯化动物是狗，证据表明， 人类可能在 30,000 年前就生活在

paragraphs.en: humans and dogs were living together maybe as long as 30,000 years ago, but the first proof is this skeleton of a Natufian woman buried with a puppy dog around 11,000 years ago. During the crisis brought by the Younger Dyras ice age, perhaps some Natufians decided to try to domesticate other animals besides dogs, like goats. Like the wheat and the dogs, the goats also became domesticated over several generations of deliberate breeding, by humans. As a result, the Natufians became not just the first farmers but also the first herders. While some Natufians headed down into the valleys and wetlands to learn how to farm,other groups may
paragraphs.zh: 一起了。但是最早的切实证据来自 于一只约 11,000 年前同纳图夫女 人合葬的小狗。 在新仙女木期带来的冰川危机期 间，也许一些纳图夫人尝试着驯服 除了狗以外的其他动物，比如山 羊。 就像小麦和狗一样，这些山羊在经 过几代的挑选与刻意繁育下被人 类驯化了。 于是，纳图夫人不仅是最早的耕 者，他们也成为了最早的牧者。 当一些纳图夫人进入河谷与湿地 学习如何耕作时，另外一群人则可 能已经进去以色列捕捉山羊了。

paragraphs.en: have gone up into the hills of Israel and captured wild goats. They killd the goats that were aggressive or too fearful,while they protected and bred those goats that were more gentle and comfotable around people. In fact,in the 1950s Russians scientists began an experiment to domesticate wild foxes.They discovered that you can after 50 years these Russian scientists have between able to creat foxes that behave just like dogs when they are around people. Domestication also changes the bodies of goats. And typically when animals become domesticated they become smaller, weaker, dumber. The same thing happened to the goats , and also the
paragraphs.zh: 他们吧那些过于好斗和过于胆怯 的杀死，对于那些温和的以及更好 的和人类相处的山羊则保护繁殖 起来。 事实上，在 20 世纪 50 年代，俄罗 斯科学家进行了一项驯化野生狐 狸的实验。他们呢发现，仅仅经过 几代，即 50 年后，他们就培育出 了像家狗一样鱼人类相处的狐狸。 驯化也改变了山羊的身体。 动物被驯化后显然变得更小、更 弱、更迟钝。山羊也一样，公山羊 的角变得更小，在人周围也表现得 更舒适。

paragraphs.en: male goats have smaller horns, and are more comfortable around people. You can see this change for yourself in these pictures. Look at how the wild goat on the left is different from the domesticated goat on the right. Archaeologist Melinda Zader has studied the domestication of goats during this time period. She says you can see the change most clearly in the the horns. Here she shows us the skull and horn of a wild goat. And here we can see the skull and horns of a domesticated goat. It is much smaller as you can see yourself.
paragraphs.zh: 你们可以从图上看到变化。 可以看到，左边这些野生山羊与右 边驯化后的山羊有多不同。 考古学家梅林达·扎德尔研究了这 段时期山羊的驯化。 她说，羊角的变化是最直观的。 这里她向我们展示了野生山羊的 头骨和角。 这里我们可以看到驯化后山羊的 头骨和角，如你所见，它们明显变 小了。

paragraphs.en: That is because, in the wild the male goats use their big horns to fight the other male goats and compete for females. But in captivity, we humans decide which male goats get to mate, and we prefer the gentle, less aggressive males, because we want to have gentle, less aggressive goats. Big horns have no use for domesticated male goats, so they shrink over time. This process of domestication began around 10,000 BC in the Middle East, but it has gone on ever since. Let's look at when we domesticated different animal species. The first was the dog, which we domesticated about 15,000 years ago, but maybe thousands of years before that. The second was probably the goat,
paragraphs.zh: 这就是因为，在野生环境中，公山 羊为了求偶，会用角来与其他公山 羊进行角斗。 但是在圈养中，是我们人类决定哪 只公山羊去配种，而我们更偏好于 温顺、不好斗的那些。大型的角对 于驯化山羊没有用，于是它们渐渐 消失了。 在中东，驯化的进程开始于约公元 前 10,000 年，但是也一直延续了 下来。让我们来看一看不同时代我 们驯化的不同物种。 最早的是狗，我们大约在 15,000 年前驯化了它们，但是也许时间还 要再往前推数千年。 第二种可能就是山羊了，我们在约

paragraphs.en: which we domesticated around 10,000 BC. Then came the pig and the sheep, both were around 9,000 BC. Cows and Cats were domesticated about a thousand years later in 8000 BC. Cats probably arrived when we first lived in towns and villages.And we stored a lot of grains which attracted the rats and the rats attracted the cats and then the cats learned to live around people. Chickens were domesticated about 2000 years after that, around 6000 BC. And finally, the horse was domesticated around 4000 BC. The horse of course would have a huge impact on communication and
paragraphs.zh: 公元前 10,000 年驯化了它们。 再然后就是猪和绵羊，我们大约在 公元前 9,000 年驯化了它们。 1000年后，大约在公元前8000年， 我们驯化了奶牛和猫。 猫应该是人类开始定居时来到人 类身边—人类储存的粮食引来老 鼠，老鼠又引来了猫。慢慢地，猫 开始留在人类身边与人类共同生 活。 那之后 2000 年，大约在公元前 6000 年，人类驯化了鸡。 最终，马在公元前 4000 年前后被 驯化。 马，在交流与战争中有着重大的影 响。但是所有这些动物都在文明的

paragraphs.en: warfare, but all of these animals have played a big role in the formation of civilization. The younger Dryas ice-age lasted about 1400 years, but then it ended quite suddenly around 9500 BC. By 8,000 BC the earth had warmed up to about where it is today, and for the last 10,000 years, the earth's temperature has been unusually warm and stable. It is this long period warmth and stability that has allowed us humans to develop civilization after 200,000 years as hunter gatherers. Now the world became warm and wet again, and the Natufians had gained skill both as farmers and as herders. By this point they had domesticated
paragraphs.zh: 形成中发挥着重要的作用。 新仙女木期的冰川时代持续了约 1400 年，在公元前 9500 年戛然而 止。 在公元前 8000 年左右，地球渐渐 回暖升温到现在的水平，在随后的 10,000 年，地球的温度逐渐趋于 温暖和稳定。 在做了狩猎采集者 200,000 年后， 正是这段温度长时期的温暖和稳 定，我们人类才得以开始发展文 明。 现在，气候又开始变得温暖湿润， 纳图夫人已经获得了耕和牧的技 巧。 他们已经驯化了8种不同作物和2

paragraphs.en: 8 different crops and 2 different animals, and conditions were perfect for this new way of life to spread. Now there was plenty of food, and these farming communities began to grow from settlements of less than 100 people into real towns with over 1000 people living together. This was the first time in history so many humans ever began living in one place. So please join me next time as we discuss three of these very early human towns. Thank you very much.
paragraphs.zh: 种不同动物，条件也都利于这种新 的生活方式的发展。 现在有了充足的食物，这些农业社 区从不到 100 人的聚居点开始发 展成为真正的城镇，有超过 1000 人居住在一起。 这么多人共同生活，这在历史上还 是第一次。 所以请一起收听下节课吧。我们将 讨论其中三个最早期的城镇。谢 谢。

titleZh: 第十四讲：历史上最古老城镇---杰里科的故事

paragraphs.en: Welcome back. Last time we talked about plants and animals domestication and how these made farming and herding possible. Farming and herding made living in towns possible, so today I’m going to tell you about three of these oldest and most important towns. One of the oldest of these towns, if not the oldest town in the world is Jericho. Jericho had been a popular hunting camp for the Natufians for thousands of years because there was a spring nearby which provided good water. A spring is a place where fresh water from underneath the earth comes up to the surface. In fact the spring still exists today. It
paragraphs.zh: 欢迎回来。上一讲我们讲述了植物 和动物的驯化以及驯化是如何让 农作和放牧成为可能。耕作与放牧 使得在城镇中生活成为可能。那么 我们今天将要讲的就是那其中三 个最古老最重要的城镇。 即使不是世界上最古老的城镇，杰 里科也是这些城镇中最古老的城 市之一。 几千年来，由于附近有一处供应良 好的泉水作为水源，杰里科一直是 纳图夫人的热门狩猎营地。泉水， 即是从地下涌到地表的活水。 事实上，如左图所见，这处泉水到

paragraphs.en: is called the Ein-es Sultan spring, and there is a picture of it on the left. This spring has been giving people water for at least 12000 years. When the Younger Dryas ended around 9500 BC, several families moved near the spring and set up a village. The village was located on Tes-es Sultan Hill, close to the spring. The Natufians began to farm the area around the hill, and now they began to build permanent houses out of stones and mud. These were all starting around 9500 BC. These houses were circular in shape. Here in this picture is a modern recreation of one of these houses. By 9400 BC there may have been as many as 70 houses in the area.
paragraphs.zh: 现在还存在着，它被叫做苏丹泉, 该泉水已经至少为人类提供了 12000 年的水。 当新仙女木期于约公元前 9500 年 结束时，一些人家搬到泉水附近并 开辟了村庄。 村庄就在苏丹山，泉水附近。 纳图夫人开垦了山附近的土地，现 在他们开始用泥土和石头来建造 耐久的房屋。 这些房子是圆形的。如图，这是一 张现代复原图。 随着公元前 9400 年，这片区域有 着约 70 座房子，在当时应当是一

paragraphs.en: Which is small to you and me but would have been a huge community at the time. Jericho thrived off of farming and herding and the population exploded. This went on for 1400 years and by the year 8000 BC the population of Jericho may have been as high as 2000 people, and the town was about 40,000 square meters in size. This is about the same size as 6 soccer fields. Around the year 8000 BC, the people of Jericho built a wall around their town. It was 3.6 meters tall and 1.8 meters wide. This is the oldest walled community in the world, and it represents a large amount of work. It would have
paragraphs.zh: 个大的社区了。 杰里科因农耕和放牧兴盛起来，人 口迅速得到增长。 这持续了大约 1400 年，到了公元 前 8000 年，杰里科的人口可能到 了约 2000 人之多，城镇的面积达 到了约 40,000 平方米。 这大约是 6 个足球场的面积。 在公元前 8000 年前后，杰里科人 在城镇周围筑了一道墙。墙有 3.6 米高，1.8 米宽。 这是世界上最早的有围墙的社区， 也是相当大的工程量。它的建造至 少要 100 人用 100 天才能完成。

paragraphs.en: taken 100 men at least 100 days of work to build this wall. The tower was 8.5 meters tall and had a staircases inside with 22 stone steps that let you climb to the top. This was the very first stone tower in history,or at least it was the first one we know about.And it was the tallest man-made structure in the world at least for thousands of years. So here is a picture of the remains of the actual tower in the picture on the right. And here is another picture of the tower to give you a better sense of size. On the left is an illustration of the tower's design. The tower is buried today for an
paragraphs.zh: 这座塔高 8.5 米，塔内还有由 22 级的石阶组成的楼梯，方便人们攀 爬至顶。 这是历史上第一座古塔，或者至少 是我们已知的第一座。并且在长达 数千年的时间里，它也是世界上最 高的人造结构。 右边的图片展示的是现实中塔的 遗迹。 右图是该塔的实图，另一张图则可 以帮助更好的理解塔的尺寸。 左图是塔的设计图。 塔现在由于很有趣的原因被埋起

paragraphs.en: interesting reason.' Ancient Middle-Eastern cities like Jericho were built mainly out of mud and stone. Over time all houses eventually fall down, or get worn down by the wind and rain. So people will live in the house, they might lived it there for a hundred or two hundred years, and then they have to rebuild the house cause it’ll falls down and they rebuild it. Instead of carrying the dirt from the old house out of the city, people would just bring in new mud bricks and build another house on top of the old one. And they also might think about people come back from working in the fields and they are covered with dust like shake the dust off themselves you know, clean them
paragraphs.zh: 来了。 古中东像杰里科一样的城镇主要 是用石头和泥土建造的。随着时间 推移所有的房子都不是倒塌就是 被风雨侵蚀。 人们不是把旧房子里的泥土带出 城市，而只是带来新的泥砖，并在 旧房屋的顶部建造另一栋房屋。 几千年来，所有这些房屋的土基都 在堆积起来，城市慢慢变得越来越 高，直到它坐落在人造小山的顶 部，称为“人造土丘”。

paragraphs.en: off , there was also garbage, so over thousands of years the earth from all of those houses and all of those people and all of the day the garbage like builds up and the town slowly gets higher and higher until its sitting on-top of a man-made hill called a 'Tell.' This is a picture of a 'Tell' in Syria. That is not a natural hill, it was made by people over thousands of years as people lived in that spot and kept building and rebuilding their houses. Here is another tell, in the Syrian city of Aleppo. In the middle ages armies would built castles on top of these tells because the high ground makes them easier to defend. The Greeks were the first people we
paragraphs.zh: 这是“人造土丘”的一张图。它并 不是自然形成的山，而是人们数千 年来居住于此并不断建造和再建 的房子。 这是另一个人造土丘，位于叙利亚 的阿勒颇。 在中世纪军队会在这些人造土丘 上建起城堡，因为高地会更容易防 守。 希腊人在公元前 300 年在小山上

paragraphs.en: know that first built a fortress on that hill around the year 300 BC, to me,300 BC seems like a long time ago, but the tell that they built the fortress on was already at least 3000 years old when the Greeks first arrived. Here is another tell,in the Syrian city of Aleppo. In the middle ages armies would built castles on the top these tells because the high ground makes them easier to defend. So the original tower and walls of Jericho are underground because over thousands of years the city grew up over them. In any case, this is the oldest walled city and stone tower on Earth. Here's another picture and illustration of the tower and wall of
paragraphs.zh: 建起堡垒，但是人造土丘某种意义 上已经至少出现了 3000 年了。 这是另外一个人造土丘，位于叙利 亚的阿勒颇。 在中世纪军队会在这些人造土丘 上建起城堡，因为高低更容易防 守。 所以杰里科的塔和墙最初是在地 下的，因为经过数千年城市在他们 之上建立起来。 总之，这是地球上最古老的有围墙 的城市和石塔。 这是杰里科塔和墙的另一张图片。 墙有 3.6 米高，我 185cm，可以想

paragraphs.en: Jericho. So the wall is 3.6 meters tall and I am about 185cm, you can imagine that the wall is twice as high as me, and you can imagine that the tower is being like four times or five times as tall as I am. Some people think the walls may have been built mainly to protect against flooding. But the most common explanation is that wealth and possessions had been accumulating in Jericho for 1500 years by the time the walls were built. It must have seemed like a rich place to the rest of the world. So the people living in Jericho probably decided they needed to build defenses to protect their homes and property from other people and from wild animals.
paragraphs.zh: 象墙是我的两倍高，而塔则大概有 我的四倍或五倍那么高。 一些人认为这些墙的建造主要是 来抵御洪水的。 但是更常见的解释则是，杰里科在 墙建立起后的 1500 年持续积累着 财富和财产。 杰里科对于世界其他地方来说一 定是个富足之地，因此杰里科人才 会决定建起墙来保护自己的家园 和财产免被其他人或野兽侵扰。

paragraphs.en: So Jericho is a good example of this transition from tiny settlements into full-sized towns. And all of this was only possible because of farming. Another important place from this time period is Gobekli Tepe, a few hundred miles north of Jericho. As you can see, Gobekli Tepe is now located in southern Turkey, very close to the Syrian border. The site has been known about since the 1960s, but in excavation began in earnest only in 1993 under the direction of the archaeologist Klaus Schmidt. As they dug, the archaeologists discovered an astonishing site. There were concentric rings of stone walls, and giant stone monoliths.
paragraphs.zh: 因此，杰里科就是从小规模的定居 点向全面城镇过渡的一个很好的 例子。 所有这些都是由于耕作。 另外一个该时期重要的地点是哥 贝克力石阵，它位于杰里科北几百 公里外。 如你所见，哥贝克力石阵位于南土 耳其，距离叙利亚边境很近。 这个地方自20世纪60年代以来就 被发现了，但是在 1993 年，在考 古学家克劳斯施密特的指导下才 开始认真地进行发掘。 在发掘时，考古学家发现了很惊人 的景观。 它有着同心圆的石墙和巨石。

paragraphs.en: And these had been built around the same time as Jericho. The site featured dozens of giant stone pillars that were carved with animal shapes. These pillars were 6 meters tall and weighed over 10,000 kilograms each. And yet they were all built with stone tools, 5 thousand years before the discovery of metal. The discovery of a building project that was this big, this complicated and this old astonished the scientists. We think that Gobekli Tepe was the world's very first temple. And it is perhaps the first large building in human history. Here is a picture of Gobekli Tepe as
paragraphs.zh: 这些也是在杰里科同时期建造的。 这处景观有数十个雕有动物形状 的石柱。 每根石柱高 6 米，重量超过 1000 千克。 在金属被发现的 5000 年前，这些 都是用石头建造的。 这处建筑的发现是惊人的，如此复 杂、年代又如此久远，这使得科学 家为之惊叹。 我们认为哥贝克力石阵是世界上 最早的神庙。 它也可能是人类历史上最早的大 型建筑。 这是一幅描绘了哥贝克力石阵建

paragraphs.en: it would have appeared when it was being built and used. This building required at least 500 people to construct. It astonished historians and completely upended our understanding of what stone-age humans were capable of doing. The third site I'd like to mention today is 'Jeff-al-ahmar' (Jerf el Ahmar) Here is a picture of the excavation. Although not as famous as Jericho or Gobekli Tepe, it is important for another reason. It teaches us many things about how humans started living together in complex farming villages. Jeff-al-ahmar' (Jerf el Ahmar) is located on the Euphrates river in
paragraphs.zh: 造和使用的图片。 该建筑需要至少 500 人来建造。 它震惊了历史学家，并且完全颠覆 了我们对于新石器时代人类所能 为之的理解。 我今天想提到的第三处景观是“杰 夫艾哈迈尔”。 这是发掘图。尽管它不像哥贝克力 石阵那样出名，由于另一个原因它 也很重要。 它教会了我们关于人类如何在复 杂的农村开始聚居生活。 “杰夫艾哈迈尔”位于叙利亚北部 的幼发拉底河。

paragraphs.en: northern Syria. In 1991, the Syrian government began to build Tishrin dam on the river, very close to the ruins of the village. That meant that the site would be flooded in 1999 and lost forever. So an emergency dig was organized in 1995 as a joint French-Syrian effort to excavate and document as much of the village as possible before the rising water destroyed it. This effort was led by the French archaeologist Danielle Stordeur. Stordeur believes that the biggest building in Jerf el-Ahmar was too big to be a house, so she thinks it was a public granary and temple. Which would make it the second communal building in history, after
paragraphs.zh: 在 1991 年，叙利亚政府开始在河 上非常靠近现存村庄的地方建蒂 什林水坝。 这意味着，这里将在 1999 年被淹 没，并且永远消失。 因此在 1995 年组织进行了紧急发 掘，法国和叙利亚的联合发掘，在 淹没水被摧毁之前尽可能多地挖 掘和记录村庄。 这项工程由法国考古学家丹尼尔 斯托德尔领导。 斯托德尔相信，杰夫艾哈迈尔最大 的建筑是粮仓和神庙。这也将其使 成为继哥贝克力石阵之后历史上 第二大的建筑。

paragraphs.en: Gobekli Tepe. This also shows us that people were beginning to organize themselves into groups bigger than families. They were starting to live in complex societies with leaders and organizations. Jeff-al-ahmar' (Jerf el Ahmar) also teaches us something about the history of houses. As I mentioned before, the first permanent houses built by the Natufians were round structures, like this one. At Jeff-al-ahmar, we see the same round design being used at first. But later people began to build rectangular rooms inside of their oval houses.
paragraphs.zh: 这也向我们表明，人们开始将自己 组织成比家庭更大的群体。 他们 开始与领导和组织一起生活在复 杂的社会中。 杰夫艾哈迈尔也帮助我们窥见房 屋历史的一角。 就如我之前提到的，纳图夫人建造 的第一批永久性房屋是圆形结构， 就像这个一样。 在杰夫艾哈迈尔，我们也看到了同 一开始一样的设计。 但后来人们开始在他们的椭圆形 房屋内建造长方形房间。

paragraphs.en: And then people began building completely rectangular houses. So if you've ever wondered about things like. 'When did people start living in houses' 'Why are houses rectangle shaped' 'When did people start making buildings other than houses.' These are the exact questions that the people at Jeff-al-ahmar were trying to answer, except they were some of the very first people in history to think about these problems. So if we take a step back and look at everything we've just learned, we can see that this period in history from 10,000 to 8,000 BC contains a lot of 'firsts'.
paragraphs.zh: 然后人们开始建造长方形的房子。 所以如果你曾好奇过，比如 人们什么时候开始住在房子里的 为什么房子是长方形的 什么时候人们开始建造除房屋以 外的建筑物？ 这些正是杰夫艾哈迈尔的人们试 图回答的问题，当然他们也是历史 上第一批思考这些问题的人。 所以如果我们回过头来看我们学 的所有东西，我们可以看到，这段 从公元前 10000 年到公元前 8000 年的历史有着很多的“第一”。

paragraphs.en: 1) The First Farmers 2) The First Herders 3) The First Temple 4) The First Stone Houses and Public Buildings 5) The First Towns 6) The First stone walls and towers. And yet we are still in the stone age. Metal tools will not be developed for another 5,000 years. And yet, although we are in the stone age, human beings have started living in a fundamentally different way. They've started living the way that we live now: getting their food from farms, and living in the cities and towns. For these reasons historians classify this period as belonging to a new era in the history of the stone age.
paragraphs.zh: 1）最早的农耕者 2）最早的牧者 3）第一座神庙 4）第一座石制房子和公共建筑 5）第一座城镇 6）第一面石墙和第一座石塔 到这里，我们仍然在石器年代， 5000 年内，还不会有金属工具被 开发出来。 但是人们已经开始以完全不同的 方式生活了,他们已经开始以我们 这样的方式来生活了：从农场获取 食物，居住在城镇中。 因此历史学家们归类了这段时期， 将其划入石器时代的历史新纪元。

paragraphs.en: They call this period the 'Neolithic' or 'New Stone Age' because of all these big changes taking place. And I think the Neolithic is fascinating time. But there's a lot to say about it, and I don't want to tire you out by trying to tell you everything at once. So we will stop our story there for now. Please join me next time as we continue to explore the Neolithic era.
paragraphs.zh: 这段时期被叫做“石器时代”或“新 石器时代”，因为在其中所有的改 变都发生着。 我认为新石器时代是非常有趣的。 但是仍有很多可以讲的，而我也不 打算一下子全盘托出，所以我们今 天就到这里。 欢迎收听我的下次课程，我们继续 探索新石器时代吧。

titleZh: 第十五讲：发生在 10000 年前的一桩少女“谋杀”案

paragraphs.en: Hello everyone ! Welcome back to our lecture series on Human Origins. In the last class I talked to you about 3 important early Neolithic sites: Jericho, Jeff al Ahkmar (Jerf el Ahmar), and Gobekli Tepe. I'd like to now take another look at Jeff al Ahkmar. This was one of the very first complex agricultural towns, and it thrived from 9200 to 8700 BC. Last time I mentioned a large building which was thought too large to be a private house and is thought to be one of the first communal buildings in history. Well Danielle Stordeur (the leading archaeologist for that site) also
paragraphs.zh: 年前的一桩少女“谋杀”案 大家好！欢迎回到我们的“人类起 源”系列讲座。 上一讲我提到了三个新石器时代 的重要遗迹：杰里科、杰夫艾哈迈 尔，以及哥贝克利。 现在让我们再次将目光对准杰夫 艾哈迈尔。 这是最早的成规模的农业城镇之 一，它兴盛于公元前 9200 年— 上一讲我提到过一座大型建筑。这 座建筑太过庞大，不可能是私人房 屋。人们认为这是史上最早的公共 建筑之一。 威尔·丹尼尔·斯托德（该遗迹的 考古学家领头人）也宣称在城镇的

paragraphs.en: reported finding a 2nd younger communal building at another part of the town. The building must have had some sort of ceremonial or religious significance because the archaeologists found large bull horns, upside-down human figures carved into a bench, and carved vultures looking over it all. They also found several stone tokens with abstract symbols inscribed into them. These must also have had important symbolic meaning to the residents of Jeff al Ahkmar. One of these stone tokens has a picture of a desert fox and of a vulture inscribed on it. Pay attention to that vulture symbol,
paragraphs.zh: 另一部分又发现了两座年代较近 的公共建筑。 这座建筑一定有着某种仪式功能 或宗教意义，因为考古学家在这里 找到了巨大的公牛角，刻在座椅上 的上下颠倒的人像，还有俯瞰这一 切的秃鹫雕塑。 他们还发现了几枚刻有抽象符号 的石头标记。 这些徽章对杰夫艾哈迈尔的居民 一定有着重要的象征意义。 其中一枚石头徽章带有沙漠之狐 的图案，还刻着一只秃鹫。 请注意这个秃鹫的图案，我们稍后

paragraphs.en: we will be seeing it again. And strangest and most ominous of all, in this building they found the crushed skeleton of a 15 year-old girl. For some reason she had died in the middle of the building, and had lain there long enough for her limbs to go stiff with rigor mortis. Somebody had also removed this girl's head shortly after her death, but left the body just lying there. And then a few hours after that, the roof of the building caught fire and collapsed on her, burying her in ash. The archaeologists had stumbled upon a 9,000 year-old murder mystery. The layer of ash which covered the
paragraphs.zh: 还会再见到它。 最离奇也最不祥之处在于，人们在 这栋建筑里发现了一具破损的 15 岁女孩的骸骨。 某种原因她死在了建筑中央，并在 那里被停放了很久，四肢都僵硬 了。 女孩死后不久，有人取走了她的头 颅，但是把她的身体留在了那里。 又过了几个小时，建筑的屋顶失 火，坍塌在女孩身上，将她埋在了 灰烬中。 考古学家偶然发现了这场 9000 年 前的悬疑命案。 骸骨上覆盖的灰烬说明这栋建筑

paragraphs.en: skeleton indicated that this building caught fire, and was never rebuilt after it collapsed upon the corpse of that poor girl. Whatever happened, the story of Jeff al Akhmar ended with violence and fire. When I first heard about the girl skeleton with the missing head, I thought it must have been cut off in an attack. But the truth may be a bit more complicated. The answer to this mystery may lie in putting together evidence from several other sites. So let's learn more about the people of this time and place, and then we will return to the mystery of Jeff al-Akhmar.
paragraphs.zh: 着了火，坍塌在了这个可怜的女孩 身上，但之后再未重建。 无论事情的真相如何，杰夫艾哈迈 尔的故事以凶杀和火灾告终了。 当我第一次听说这具无头女尸，我 认为她一定是遇袭并被斩首。 然而真相可能更加复杂。 这个谜团的答案或许隐藏在其他 几处遗迹所拼凑出的证据中。 那么就让我们多了解一下彼时彼 处的人们，之后我们再回到杰夫艾 哈迈尔之谜。

paragraphs.en: The answer to our mystery may lie in discoveries made at another ancient community called Chatal-Hayuk. (Çatalhöyük) This town is located a few hundred miles to the west of Jeff al-Akhmar, in what is now southern Turkey. This is an artists depiction of Chatal-Hayuk at its peak. This community flourished between 7500 and 5700 BC. If you are paying close attention to the dates, you might notice that this town is about 2000 years younger than Jericho, Jeff al-Akhmar, or Gubekli-Tepe. But in Chatal-Hayuk, we have also discovered these ceremonial bull horns in several rooms. These are in slightly better
paragraphs.zh: 而这个谜团的答案可能就藏在另 外一处名为加泰土丘的古代群落 的考古发现中。 这座城镇位于杰夫艾哈迈尔以西 几百英里处，这里如今是土耳其南 部。 这是加泰土丘的鼎盛时期的艺术 构想图。 这个群落繁盛于公元前 如果你留意了时间，你或许会注意 到，这座城镇要比杰里科、杰夫艾 哈迈尔，以及哥贝克利晚了约 但是在加泰土丘，我们也在几个房 间中发现了这些仪式用的公牛角。 这些牛角的保存状况稍好一些。

paragraphs.en: condition. Here is another photograph depicting some of these horns. In both Chatal-Hayuk and Jeff al-Akhmar, bull horns seem to be an important religious symbol. Here is an artists depiction of what the room with the horns would have looked like when it was in use. Notice the horns, the handprints on the wall, the painting of a bull, and the human skulls. This was a room with clear religious and symbolic meaning to the people of Chatal-Hayuk. Here is another room from Chatal-Hayuk. And look closely, do you notice anything.
paragraphs.zh: 这是另一副展示这些牛角的照片。 在加泰土丘和杰夫艾哈迈尔，牛角 似乎都是重要的宗教象征。 这是艺术家所描绘的摆放着牛角 的房间曾经的样子。 请注意这些牛角、墙上的手印、牛 的画像，还有人的头骨。 这间房间对加泰土丘人具有明显 的宗教及象征意义。 这是另一间加泰土丘的房间。 近距离观察一下，你注意到什么了 吗？

paragraphs.en: Here let me show you the drawings again, but this time as they would have appeared, painted with red paint on plaster wall. Although Chatal Hayuk and Jeff al-Akhmar were separated by around 2000 years in time, they share some common symbols. To my eyes the vultures painted on the wall at Chatal Hayuk, and the vultures on the stone from Jeff al-Akhmar seem very similar. Perhaps this is not a coincidence, and the two towns shared a common religion in which vultures played a big role. Look more closely at those Chatal Hayuk vultures, do you see the headless human bodies painted next to them? Perhaps that is another clue to the skeleton at Jeff al-Akhmar.
paragraphs.zh: 我再给你们展示一下这些绘画，但 是这次它们是用红色颜料画在石 膏墙上的。 尽管加泰土丘和杰夫艾哈迈尔相 隔了 2000 年，他们仍然有一些共 同的象征物。 在我看来，加泰土丘墙上画着的秃 鹫和杰夫艾哈迈尔石头上刻着的 秃鹫是非常相似的。 也许这不是巧合，而是因为两个城 镇有着共同的宗教信仰，并且秃鹫 在其中有着重要意义。 请再仔细观察一下加泰土丘的秃 鹫，看到旁边画着的无头人身了 吗？

paragraphs.en: You see,to me at least it seems plausible that Jeff al-Akhmar, Chatal-Huyuk and Gubekli Tepe all shared a common religious culture. They are close enough that people could travel between them on foot. For example, From Gobekli Tepe to Jerf el Ahmar, it is 198 kilometers. It would take about 1 week to make that trip on foot. Likewise, it is 678 kilometers from Gobekli Tepe to Chatal Hayuk. That would take about 3 weeks of walking. These are long trips for an individual to make, but not very far at all for a culture or an idea or a trade network to spread. One plausible interpretation of this evidence is that this culture might
paragraphs.zh: 如你所见，至少对我而言可能杰夫 艾哈迈尔、加泰土丘和哥贝克利都 有共同的宗教文化。 这些地方距离很近，步行即可来 往。 例如，哥贝克利和杰夫艾哈迈尔相 距 198 千米，大约一周的时间可以 走完这段路程。 与之类似，哥贝克利和加泰土丘相 距 678 千米，需要 3 周可以走完这 段路程。 这样的距离对个人而言是长途跋 涉，但对于文化和思想的传播却是 微不足道的。 对这些证据有一个可靠的解释：这 一宗教文化或许对逝者进行某种

paragraphs.en: have had a special ritual for the dead. First the family would remove the head from the body. Then the body would be left outside where the vultures could eat it. While you or I might find this a little strange, this practice of exposing a body to vultures is still performed today. It is called a “Sky Burial” and it is a tradition in parts of Tibet. It is also performed by the Zoroastrian religion in Iran and India. Perhaps this is the beginning of that tradition in human history. This may also explain the purpose of the Gobekli Tepe temple.
paragraphs.zh: 特殊的仪式。 首先家人会将死者的头部取走。 之后尸体会被留在室外，让秃鹫来 啄食。 这在我们看来似乎有点不可思议， 然而这种将遗体留给秃鹫的做法 如今仍然存在。 这叫“天葬”，是西藏部分地区的 传统。 并且这种做法还存在于伊朗和印 度的拜火教中。 也许这就是人类历史上这一传统 的起源。 这也解释了哥贝克利的庙宇存在 的意义。

paragraphs.en: Since the temple had no roof, maybe it was the place where people performed ritual sky burials. The bodies were left next-to or on-top of those carved stones. Then the vultures would come down and eat the flesh, which would free the dead persons soul and allow them to ascend into heaven or the afterlife. So this might explain why the skeleton at Jeff al-Akhmar had no head. When I first saw the skeleton, I thought some enemy must have killed her and cut off her head as an insult. But maybe there was some kind of disaster which killed her, like an attack, or an earthquake, or a fire.
paragraphs.zh: 由于庙宇没有屋顶，也许这就是人 们进行天葬的地方。 遗体就被放置在这些石刻上方或 旁边。 随后秃鹫会降临，吃掉血肉，以将 逝者的灵魂从肉体中释放升天。 所以这或许解释了为何杰夫艾哈 迈尔的骸骨没有头颅。 当我第一次见到这具骸骨时，我以 为是某些仇敌杀死了她并通过斩 首来羞辱死者。 然而她也可能是死于某种意外灾 祸，比如疾病发作、地震，或者火 灾。

paragraphs.en: And then in the middle of the disaster, her family didn't have time to move her whole body, so instead they just cut off her head to make sure that her soul could escape her body and get into heaven. So instead of enemies removing her head as an act of hate, maybe her family removed her head as an act of love. We must always be careful about our interpretations, because our modern assumptions can change the way we understand evidence. Now, nothing that I had told you is proven, all of this is speculation, but point is I hope it makes you interested in this time period, and interested in the work of these archaeologies so that you’ll take the time and do some reading for
paragraphs.zh: 在灾难中，她的家人没能来得及带 走她的整个遗体，所以他们只能割 下她的头颅以确保她的灵魂能得 以飞升。 因此，并没有什么因恨将她斩首的 仇敌，只有爱着她的家人。 我们在解读历史时一定要谨慎，因 为我们现代的揣测会改变我们对 证据的理解方式。 我所讲述的这些都仍未经证实，全 部都仍是假说，但重要的是希望你 们对这个时代感兴趣，然后你们去 自己拓展阅读，在你的检视中进行 不同的阐释。

paragraphs.en: yourself, maybe you will come up with a different interpretation once you examine the evidence. So now that we've learned a bit about the religious believes of these people from the early Neolithic and can make better informed guesses about what happens to that poor girl of Jeff al Ahkmar. Let's stop here, and then we will continue next time and as we examine two more Neolithic communities and the discovery of a new building which will change the world. Thank you very much.
paragraphs.zh: 所以我们已经了解到新石器时代 的宗教信仰，也大致对杰夫艾哈迈 尔的小女孩产生了更具化的猜想， 我们今天的课就到这里。在下一讲 我们将继续对新石器时代重要的 另外两个社会和一座改变世界的 建筑进行讲解。谢谢。

paragraphs.en: Lecture Welcome back, let us begin this lesson by returning to Chatal-Huyuk and interesting place that deserves some more of our time. Around the time that people were building Chatal Huyuk, they also made a big discovery. They learned that if you roast limestone in a fire you will create a new substance called quicklime. Chemically what happens is the fire drives off the carbon dioxide and turns Calcium Carbonate into Calcium Oxide. Then they discovered that if you add water to the quicklime it will steam and give off heat, and then it will turn into a new substance which
paragraphs.zh: 年前的人类生活： 欢迎回来，现在让我们回到加泰土 丘，这可是个有趣的地方，值得我 们多花些时间了解一番。 在人们建造加泰土丘时，他们还有 了一项重大发现。 他们发现将石灰岩在火上烤可以 制造一种新的物质，这种物质叫生 石灰。 火焰通过化学反应可以驱除二氧 化碳，将碳酸钙转化为氧化钙。 后来他们又发现如果生石灰掺水 会蒸腾发热，之后又会变成一种新 的物质—— 熟石灰。

paragraphs.en: called slaked lime. Chemically the water hydrates Calcium Oxide and creates Calcium Hydroxide. Finally, the slaked lime reacts with the Carbon Dioxide in the air and turns back into limestone. Chemically we say the Calcium Hydroxide is carbonated and turns back into Calcium Carbonate. So you see this is a cycle. With just heat and water you can turn limestone (which is a very common rock) into three different materials. And since slaked lime is a soft material that hardens back into limestone, it is a very useful building material.
paragraphs.zh: 氧化钙与水化合，生成了氢氧化 钙。 最终，熟石灰与空气中的二氧化碳 发生反应，又会变回石灰岩。 在化学上，我们称这种反应为氢氧 化钙碳化，还原成碳酸钙。 所以你可以看出这是一个循环。 仅仅靠火和水你就能将石灰岩（这 是一种非常常见的岩石）变成三种 不同的物质。 而由于熟石灰是一种柔软的材料， 并且还能硬化变回石灰岩，它成了 一种非常实用的建筑材料。

paragraphs.en: In fact we call this the 'lime cycle' and it was discovered by the people of the Middle East around 7000 BC. Now if you add some sand to the slaked lime you create a wonderful building material called lime mortar or lime plaster. Its basically two names for the same thing. You can use this material to plaster surfaces, or to hold bricks and stones together. The Neolithic people loved plaster. After it was discovered, they used it all the time on the insides and outside of their buildings. Now let's go back to Chatal-Hayuk and see how they made use of this material.
paragraphs.zh: 实际上我们将这个现象称为“石灰 循环”，这是由公元前 7000 年生 活在中东的人发现的。 如果你向熟石灰中再掺一些沙子， 你就得到了一种绝佳的建筑材料， 叫做石灰砂浆或石灰泥。 基本上这就是同一种物质的两个 不同名称。 你可以用这种材料涂抹墙面，或者 将砖块和石头固定在一起。 新石器时代的人很爱用这种灰泥 抹墙。 他们发现了这种建筑材料后，就把 它用在了建筑的里里外外。 现在我们回到加泰土丘来看看他 们是如何利用这种材料的。

paragraphs.en: Here again is a depiction of Çatalhöyük. Look closely at the town. Here is another depiction. Unlike Jericho Catal, Hayuk has no defensive wall. Instead all of the houses are connected to each other, and the outer houses formed the exterior wall for the whole town. As you can see there are no windows or doors on the houses that are on the edge of town. In Çatalhöyük, social life was conducted on the roofs. People would walk around from roof to roof, talking and socializing and working. Then they would climb down
paragraphs.zh: 这是一幅描绘加泰土丘的图片。请 仔细观察这座城镇。 这是另一幅画像。 与杰利科不同，加泰土丘没有围 墙。 取而代之的是，所有的房屋都互相 连接，外面的房屋就形成了整个村 庄的围墙。 你可以看到，房屋外围是没有窗户 或者门的。 在加泰土丘，社会生活是在屋顶开 展的。 人们会在屋顶间走动、交谈、社交， 以及工作。 之后他们会通过梯子爬下来，回到

paragraphs.en: ladders into their own homes and take part in their private lives there. Here is a re-creation of what one of the Çatalhöyük houses may have looked like on the inside. Notice the heavy use of plaster on the interior walls. I really like this house and the feeling of domestic space that it creates. Here is another recreation. Again notice that ladder which leads to the roofs and public spaces in the town. Notice the heavy use of plaster on the interior walls. Çatalhöyük is a major dig site today in modern Turkey. Excavation is still ongoing.
paragraphs.zh: 自己家中，投入到各自的私人生活 中。 这是一幅加泰土丘的复原图，展示 了其曾经可能有过的风貌。 请注意房屋内墙上涂抹的大量灰 泥。 我真的非常喜欢这栋房子，以及它 营造出的居家空间。 这是另一幅复原图。 请注意通往屋顶及城镇公共空间 的梯子。 依然请注意房屋内墙上涂抹的大 量灰泥。 加泰土丘是如今土耳其的一处主 要考古发掘遗迹。 发掘工作仍在进行当中。

paragraphs.en: Here we can see workers digging at the site. Since it is such an important location, a giant roof has been built over the entire dig site to protect it from the weather. Now let us take a look at the third community in this region:'Ain Ghazal. This town was discovered in 1974 when the Jordanian government was building a new highway. It is located near the Zarqa River. And here is an artists depiction of the town. 'Ain Ghazal flourished from around 7250 BC until 5000 BC. This excavation was led by professor Gary Rollerson, and as in
paragraphs.zh: 在这张照片中我们能看到工作人 员在现场进行考古发掘。 由于这是一个非常重要的考古遗 迹，整个发掘现场上方修建了一个 巨大的顶棚，以使其免遭恶劣天气 的破坏。 现在我们再看看另一处位于该地 区的群落：艾因加扎勒。 这座城镇发现于 1974 年，当时约 旦政府正在修建一条新的高速公 路。 它位于扎尔卡河附近。 这是艺术家笔下这座城镇的风貌。 艾因加扎勒兴盛于约公元前 这场考古发掘是由加里·罗尔森教 授主导的，在其他地方，他还发现

paragraphs.en: other places he discovered more of these skeletons with their heads removed. But at 'Ain Ghazal, they did something new and interesting with the heads. First the body would be left outside the town, where it was exposed to the vultures. Then the skull would be removed from the rest of the the body. Plaster was then added to the skull. And a sculpture of the dead person's face was created in plaster around the dead person’s skull. Then cowrie shells were added to represent their eyes. Here is what cowrie shells look like. By the time it was finished, the artist
paragraphs.zh: 了更多的无头骸骨。 但是在艾因加扎勒，人们对头颅做 了些有趣的新尝试。 首先遗体会被留在城镇外面，暴露 给秃鹫。 接着头骨会被取下。 然后在头骨上涂抹灰泥。 这样就造出了逝者的面部模型。 接下来用玛瑙贝做成眼睛。 玛瑙贝就是这个样子的。 当其最后完工时，艺术家已经在逝

paragraphs.en: had created a plaster sculpture of the dead person's head built around their actual skull. Archaeologists have un-earthed many of these plaster heads at 'Ain Ghazal. We think this custom may also have existed in Jericho, but the heads are much better preserved at 'Ain Ghazal. Archaeologists theorize that this may have been a form of ancestor worship. Many of these heads were then attached to a body made out of straw and plaster. These plaster bodies are significant, because they are the first large-scale depiction of the human form. In a sense, these are the first statues
paragraphs.zh: 者本身的头骨外面用灰泥塑造了 一个头颅。 考古学家们已经发掘出了许多这 样的石膏头像。 我们认为杰利科也有这样的习俗， 但在艾因加扎勒，这些头像保存得 更好。 考古学家认为，这可能是祖先崇拜 的一种形式。 很多头像下面都会接上一个由麻 杆和灰泥混合制成的身体。 这些雕像也很重要，因为它们是对 人类形体的首次大型刻画。 从某种意义上说，这是历史上的第

paragraphs.en: in history. The picture on the right is a photograph of the famous Hellenistic statue Laocoon and His Sons. I used to think of this as an ancient statue because it was made 2200 years ago. But the 'Ain Ghazal statues are 7000 years older! However not all bodies were treated this way. According to some estimates, nearly 1/3 of all bodies were simply dumped outside on a trash heap. This may represent the emergence of different social classes at 'Ain Ghazal. Now let's take a step back and review.
paragraphs.zh: 一批雕像。 右边的图片是著名雕像《拉奥孔》。 我曾经把它想象成一座古老的雕 像，因为它是在 2200 年前制作的。 但是，艾因加扎勒雕像比它还要早 了 7000 年！ 但不是所有的尸体都能有这样的 待遇。 据推算，几乎三分之一的尸体都被 扔到了垃圾堆里。这可能代表了艾 因加扎勒的不同社会阶层的出现。 现在让我们后退一步。

paragraphs.en: So far we have now looked at 5 important Neolithic sites: Jericho, Jeff al-Akhmar, Gobekli Tepe, Chatal-Hayuk, and Ain Ghazal. The first three sites were prominent from 9000 - 7000 BC. The last two were prominent from about 7000 - 5000 BC. So roughly speaking they represent the early and later Neolithic in the Middle East. But I don't want to give you the impression that there were only 5 towns during the Neolithic period. I chose those five because they are some of the most important and famous from that time. But in reality, the discovery of farming led to population boom in
paragraphs.zh: 到目前为止，我们已经了解了 5 个重要的新石器时代遗址：杰利 科、杰夫艾哈迈尔、哥贝克利遗址、 加泰土丘和艾因加扎勒。 前三个地点在公元前 9000-7000 年都很突出。 后两个在公元前 7000-5000 年非 常突出。 粗略来说，它们代表了中东地区早 期和晚期的新石器时代。 但我不想给你们留下这样的印象： 新石器时代只有 5 个城镇。 我选择了这五个，因为它们是当时 最重要和最著名的。 但在现实中，农业的发现导致了公 元前 10,000-7000 年间中东地区

paragraphs.en: the Middle-East between 10,000 and 7,000 BC. There were at least dozens of farming towns scattered across what is now Turkey, Israel, Jordan, Syria and Iraq. I have put some of these towns on a map to give you a better sense of things. And of course these are just the towns we know about. There were probably dozens more that have never been discovered. You should think of the communities that we are discussing not as the only towns in the region, but as representatives of the bigger changes that were taking place all across the middle east at this time. Farming, Herding, and Cities are
paragraphs.zh: 的人口激增。 至少有几十个农业城镇散布在现 在的土耳其、以色列、约旦、叙利 亚和伊拉克。 我把这些城镇放到地图上，好让你 们看得更清楚。 当然，这些都是我们所知道的城 镇。可能还有几十个从未被发现。 我们所讨论的这些群体，并非当地 仅有的城镇，而是当时中东地区正 在发生剧变的代表。 农耕、放牧和城市是我们人类曾经

paragraphs.en: some of the most powerful ideas that we humans have ever had. And the thing about powerful ideas, is that they tend to spread. Around 7,000 BC, just as Çatalhöyük and ‘Ain Ghazal were getting started, the idea of farming made the jump across the Agean Sea where it spread into Greece and the Balkans. From there it began a slow and steady move into Europe. This map shows the spread of farming. And the story of how the Neolithic Revolution spread to Europe is the next step in our journey through World History. Thank you very much.
paragraphs.zh: 拥有的最强大的思想。 而强大的思想，则往往具有传播 性。 公元前 7000 年前后，就在加泰土 丘和艾因加扎勒开始发展之时，农 业的概念跨越了爱琴海，蔓延到了 希腊和巴尔干半岛。自此，它开始 缓慢而稳定地进入欧洲。 这张地图显示了农业的传播。 新石器时代革命传播到欧洲的故 事，则是我们世界历史系列讲座的 下一站旅程。 谢谢。

titleZh: 第十七讲：新石器文明的小船下海启航

paragraphs.en: Hello, my name is James and welcome back to our lecture series on World History. Today we will discuss how the Neolithic spread to Europe. Okay, let's begin. I think of the Neolithic as the time period when people had already learned to farm, but were still using stone tools. As we saw in the previous lessons, farming first developed in the part of the world that is now Israel, Jordan and Syria, about 10,000 BC. Over the next 2000 years it spread to what is now southern Turkey and Northern Iraq. By 6500 BC, farming had spread to
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，我是詹姆斯，欢迎回到我 们的世界历史讲座。 今天我们将讨论的是：新石器文明 是如何传播到欧洲的。 好的，那我们就开始吧！ 新石器时代，人们已经懂得如何去 耕作，但仍然使用石制工具。 在之前的课上我们讲到过，农耕最 早起源于约公元前 10,000 年，在 今天的以色列、约旦和叙利亚地 区。 在之后的 2000 年，农耕传播到了 现在的土耳其南部与伊拉克北部。 到了公元前 6500 年，农耕已经传

paragraphs.en: WesternAnatolia and the Nile River. This map of Europe shows you the rough extent of farming around the year 6500BC. I've also included important communities that were engaged in farming at that point. Places like 'Ain Ghazal, Jarmo, Çatalhöyük, and Hacilar. It was at this point that these first farmers began bringing farming to Europe. Now this of course brings up a question: How did these first farmers get across the water? An answer to that question can be found in a discovery that was made in the Netherlands in 1955. In 1955, the Dutch were constructing theA-28 Motorway. During this construction they had to
paragraphs.zh: 播到了西安纳托利亚和尼罗河。 这张欧洲地图粗略展示了公元前 6500 年前后的农耕程度，同时我 也加上了那时进行农耕的重要社 区，比如艾因加扎勒、耶莫， 加 泰土丘和哈吉拉尔。 也就是在这里，这些初代农民开始 将农耕带到欧洲。 那么本节课的问题自然就来了：他 们是如何跨越水域的呢？ 这个问题的答案可以从 1955 年荷 兰的一个发现中找到。 1955 年，德国正在建造 A-28 高速 公路。 这项工程需要跨越像这样的泥炭

paragraphs.en: pass through a peat bog, which probably looked a lot like this. And while they were digging up the bog they found this thing. It's a dug-out canoe, the oldest example in the world, and it is from 8000 BC. You make a dug out canoe by taking a log, putting mud around the edges, and then lighting a fire in the center. Then once the fire has burned-down you dig out the charred wood and light another fire until you have hollowed out the log. When you are done, you get a boat that looks something like this. We call it a dug-out canoe, because you "dig-out" the wood in the center of the log.
paragraphs.zh: 沼泽。 施工过程中，他们发现了这件物 品。 这是一艘世界上最古老的独木舟， 来自公元前 8000 年。 做一艘独木舟，你要找到一段圆 木，在边缘铺上泥，然后在中心点 火。 火一旦熄灭，挖掉焦木，然后再次 点火，如此这般，直到挖空木头中 心。 这些完成后，就得到了一艘这样的 独木舟。 我们叫它“独木舟”，是因为它是 挖空木头中心得到的。

paragraphs.en: These early farmers probably set out into the ocean with their animals and seeds in small boats like that. And as they moved into Europe, they brought the knowledge of farming with them. We know this was happening, because after 6500 BC we start to find evidence of farming across the water in places like Hocha Chesme. TheAgean Sea and modern-day Greece was probably the first part of Europe to learn about farming. Around 7000 BC we find evidence of farming near Franchthi cave, which had already been inhabited by people for thousands of years. The Neolithic settlement of Nea Nikomedia dates from about 6250 BC.
paragraphs.zh: 这些早期农民可能就是这样，带着 他们的动物和种子，乘着小船出发 了。 随着他们一同进入欧洲的，还有农 业知识。 我们能知道这些信息，是因为公元 前 6500 年之后，在类似霍察切什 梅这样跨水域的地区，我们发现了 农耕的证据。 爱琴海和现代希腊地区可能是欧 洲率先开始农耕的区域。 我们发现了约公元前 7000 年弗兰 克西洞穴遗址存在农耕的证据，在 那里，人类已经生活了数千年。 新尼科美亚在新石器时代的定居 点可以追溯至公元前 6250 年。

paragraphs.en: The fortified settlements of Sesklo and Dimini date from around 6000 BC. And the very first permanent settlement inAthens began around 6000 BC. People probably started by living in the caves on the acropolis, and then they began to build houses as the population grew. We see the very first settlements on Crete around 6000 BC. This settlement would eventually develop into Knossos, the capital of the Mycenean civilization. So around 6500 BC to 6000 BC, at that 500 year period, farming was spreading from Mideast into Southeastern Europe. But as they moved north of Greece, these early
paragraphs.zh: 塞斯克洛和迪米尼的筑防御工事 的定居点可以追溯到公元前 6000 年左右。 雅典最早的永久性定居点最早出 现于公元前约 6000 年。 人们可能最早住在雅典卫城的山 洞里，然后随着人口增长，他们开 始建造房屋。 我们可以看到在公元前 6000 年左 右最早的定居点克里特。 这一定居点最终发展成了迈锡尼 文明的首都克诺索斯。 所以大约在公元前 6500 年至公元 前 6000 年这 500 年间，农业从欧 洲逐渐传入了欧洲东南部。但当他 们北进到达希腊的时候，这些早期 农民遇到了麻烦。

paragraphs.en: farmers ran into a problem. In the south were large mountain ranges. And when they went north they came to other problems. Dense forests that made travel difficult, and probably scared the Middle-Eastern migrants, who had nothing like this in the Middle East. But there was one way into Europe's interior in those days. And that was by travelling along the rivers. For these Middle-Eastern migrants, the most useful of these rivers was probably the Danube, Europe's longest river. In those days the Danube was like a highway from the Black Sea into the
paragraphs.zh: 南方有大型山脉。 而当他们北进时，他们遇到了其他 麻烦。 密集的森林让旅途变得艰难，并且 可能吓到了这些从未见到如此景 致的中东移民们。 但当时，进入欧洲内陆的道路只此 一条。 那就是沿着河行进。 对于这些中东移民来说，这些河流 中最有用的要属多瑙河，它是欧洲 最长的河。 在那些日子里，多瑙河就像从黑海 进入欧洲内陆的高速公路。

paragraphs.en: interior of Europe. These first farmers probably made their way north along the shore of the Black Sea until they reached the mouth of the Danube. Then they travelled up into the interior of the Europe by going up the river in their boats.And as they did this, these farmers came into contact with the Hunter-Gatherers who were already living in Europe, and who had been living there since the end of the last ice-age. One of the most interesting of these Native European Settlements is a place called Lepinski Vir. Here is an artists illustration of what Lepinski Vir might have looked like. It was an extensive settlement on the banks of the Danube (in what is now
paragraphs.zh: 最早的农民应该是沿着黑海海岸 到达了多瑙河河口。 然后他们乘着船，沿河而上，进入 了欧洲内陆。在此过程中，他们与 当地靠狩猎采集为生的人们产生 了联系。这些原住民从上个冰河世 纪终结时，就已经在这里定居了。 其中一个最有趣的欧洲原住民定 居点就是莱本斯基维尔。 这是一幅推测的莱本斯基维尔的 复原图。 它是多瑙河岸上的一个大型定居 点（位于现在的塞尔维亚）。

paragraphs.en: modern Serbia). At this settlement hundreds of people probably lived together, and supported themselves with hunting, gathering, and especially fishing. Lepinski Vir is special because the house all had this interesting trapezoidal shape with a hearth in the center, and a floor made out of a local red-colored plaster. The walls and roofs of the houses have all decomposed over the millenia, but the floors remain. The site of Lepinski Vir was flooded by Hydro-electric dam in the mid 20th-century. But the foundations were moved hundreds of meters away, above the rising water and placed under a glass roof in a museum.
paragraphs.zh: 可能有数百人共同生活在这个定 居点，他们自给自足：打猎，采集 植物，特别是捕鱼。 莱本斯基维尔非常特别，因为房子 都是有趣的梯形，中心有一个炉 膛，地板由当地的红色石膏制成。 房子的墙和屋顶千年之间已经消 失不见，但是地面保存了下来。 莱本斯基维尔的遗址在 20 世纪中 期被水电大坝淹没。 但是，地基被移动到了数百米之 外，安置于上升的水面上，博物馆 的玻璃屋顶下。

paragraphs.en: You can visit this museum today if you ever make a trip to Serbia. Here is an artists depiction of the settlement which I really like. I think it gives you a real sense of what it must have been like to live there 8000 years ago. Nobody is really sure what the top part of the houses looked like. Some people think they were shaped like this picture. With a triangular construction. Others speculate that they may have looked more like a modern shed, as you can see in this artists illustration. Another striking find at Lepinski Vir are these statues that seem to look like people who are half-fish and half-man.
paragraphs.zh: 如果你去塞尔维亚旅行的话，可以 去博物馆参观。 这是我很喜欢的遗址复原图，它会 让你有真的住在八千多年前这里 的感觉。 没有人知道房屋的顶部是什么样 的。 有些人认为可能是图片上这 种三角形结构。 也有人则推测说可能更像是现代 的这种棚子，就像插图上的那样。 莱本斯基维尔另一项引人注目的 发现，是这些看起来像半鱼半人的 雕像。

paragraphs.en: I have no idea what these statues meant to the people of Lepinski-Vir, but I think there is something sad and haunting about the expressions on these faces. Sometimes the statues were placed next to the fire-pits at the center of the houses. Maybe they represented a god or had some sort of religious meaning to these people. Anyway, it was at Lepinski Vir that these European Hunter-Gatherers and Middle-Eastern farmers came into extensive contact. Happily, we think this first-contact was largely peaceful, and we have genetic evidence that there was extensive intermarriage between these two groups. They learned from each other, married each other, traded with each
paragraphs.zh: 我不知道这些雕像对于莱本斯基 维尔人来说意味着什么，但是我觉 得他们的脸上上有一些令人悲伤 且难以忘怀的表情。 有时雕像放在房子中央的火坑旁 边， 也许它们是神像，或具有某 种宗教意义。 总之，就是在莱本斯基维尔，这些 欧洲采集者和中东的农耕者进行 了大量的接触。 令人高兴的是，我们认为这些最初 的联系基本上是和平的，我们也有 基因证据表明两个族群间存在大 量通婚。 他们互相学习，通婚，交易，在几 个世纪后创造了一批新的种族。

paragraphs.en: other, and over a few centuries created a new people. From Lepinski Vir, this new people began to spread outwards. As you can see from the map, Lepinski Vir sits right at the mouth of the Hungarian Plain. This was probably the site of some of the first farms in central Europe. And by about 6000 BC, the middle-eastern immigrants and native Europeans had created a new culture called Danubian culture by the archaeologists. Another name for these Danubian people is the "Longhouse People." So please join me next time as we talk about these people, the first farmers of Europe, and the culture that they created.
paragraphs.zh: 这个新的种族从莱本斯基维尔向 外扩散。 从地图上我们可以看到，莱本斯基 维尔坐落在匈牙利平原的河口。 这可能是欧洲中部初代农场的一 部分。 到了大概公元前 6000 年，中东移 民和欧洲原住民创造了一种考古 学家称为多瑙河文化的新文化。 这些多瑙河人又称“长屋人”。 下节课我们将讨论这些人，最早的 欧洲农耕者和他们所创造的文明。

titleZh: 第十八讲：欧洲的新农村建设

paragraphs.en: Hello, my name is James and welcome back to our lecture series on World History. Today we will continue our discussion of the spread of farming into Europe. We will talk about the unique culture that these first European farmers created. Last time I told you that farming spread up the Danube River into the Hungarian Plain. But after that it stopped spreading northwards for almost a thousands years. The farming methods developed in the middle east could not deal with the harsh winters of Northern
paragraphs.zh: 大家好，我是詹姆斯，欢迎回到世 界历史系列讲座。 今天我们将继续讨论农业在欧洲 的发展。 我们将讨论初代欧洲农民所创造 的独特文化。 上节课说到，农业把多瑙河延伸到 匈牙利平原。 但在那之后的近千年间，传播中止 了。 中东地区的农业方法无法适应北 欧严酷的寒冬。

paragraphs.en: Europe. Eventually though, these first European Farmers figured out a solution. This involved paying very close attention to the night sky. By watching the stars carefully each night, they learned how the night sky changes over the year, and they were able to predict the changing of the seasons. They were able to develop an accurate calendar. And by paying close attention to the seasons, they learned how to time their planting and their harvesting in order to give their crops enough time to grow before the first frost. They also began to domesticate the cow, and learned how to use the cow
